title: An improved bound for the Manickam-Miklós-Singhi conjecture
overall_summary: The paper studies the Manickam-Miklós-Singhi fixed-size subset problem: for n real numbers with nonnegative total sum, how many k-element subsets must have nonnegative sum. Its main result proves that for n > k(4e log k)^k, every such collection has at least binom(n-1,k-1) nonnegative k-sums, improving the previous eventual bound for the MMS conjecture. The core method orders the numbers, introduces a notion of a central element whose every containing k-sum is nonnegative, and iteratively removes a largest noncentral element together with the smallest k-1 elements; either a central element appears and yields many nonnegative sums by replacement, or the absence of central elements forces a two-block construction producing many nonnegative sums. The paper also gives a simpler weaker proof using Baranyai’s partition theorem when the ambient size is divisible by k, plus an order-statistic inequality. For the original Bernoulli-weight problem, the main potential use is after conditioning the Bernoulli subset on its size: fixed-size uniform subset estimates can be applied to centered weights, while the complement-pair observation is directly suggestive for uniform random subsets.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Complement-pair bound for unrestricted subset sums
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X = {x_1,...,x_n} be a collection of n real numbers with total sum ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then at least 2^{n-1} subsets C ⊆ {1,...,n} have nonnegative sum ∑_{i in C} x_i ≥ 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Partition all subsets C ⊆ {1,...,n} into complementary pairs {C, {1,...,n}\C}. For each pair, the two corresponding subset sums add to the total sum ∑X, which is nonnegative. Hence in each complementary pair at least one of the two sums is nonnegative. There are 2^{n-1} complementary pairs, giving the bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Known divisible case via Baranyai partition theorem
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define A(n,k) to be the minimum, over all collections X = {x_1,...,x_n} of n real numbers with ∑X ≥ 0, of the number of k-element subsets S ⊆ {1,...,n} satisfying ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0. If k divides n, then A(n,k) = binom(n-1,k-1). Equivalently, whenever k|n and ∑X ≥ 0, at least binom(n-1,k-1) of the k-subsets have nonnegative sum, and this lower bound is best possible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The lower bound uses Baranyai’s theorem: when k|n, the complete k-uniform hypergraph {1,...,n}^{(k)} can be partitioned into parallel classes, each class consisting of n/k pairwise disjoint k-sets whose union is all of {1,...,n}. In any one parallel class, the sum of the k-sums over all blocks equals the total sum ∑X ≥ 0, so at least one block in that class has nonnegative sum. The number of parallel classes is binom(n,k)/(n/k) = (k/n)binom(n,k) = binom(n-1,k-1), yielding at least that many nonnegative k-sums. Sharpness is witnessed by taking x_1 = n-1 and x_2 = ... = x_n = -1: a k-subset has nonnegative sum exactly when it contains x_1, giving binom(n-1,k-1) such subsets.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Order-statistic inequality, Equation (2)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let x_1 ≥ x_2 ≥ ... ≥ x_n be real numbers with ∑_{i=1}^n x_i ≥ 0. Then for every integer j with 1 ≤ j ≤ n-1, one has j x_1 + (n-j)x_{j+1} ≥ 0, and therefore x_1 ≥ -((n-j)/j)x_{j+1}. More generally, the same estimate holds in any ordered subcollection Y = {y_1 ≥ ... ≥ y_N} with nonnegative total sum: y_1 ≥ -((N-j)/j)y_{j+1} for 1 ≤ j ≤ N-1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because x_1 is the largest element, the sum of the first j terms is at most j x_1. Because x_{j+1} is at least every term x_{j+1},...,x_n, the sum of the remaining n-j terms is at most (n-j)x_{j+1}. Hence ∑X ≤ jx_1 + (n-j)x_{j+1}. Since ∑X ≥ 0, the displayed upper bound must be nonnegative, giving jx_1 + (n-j)x_{j+1} ≥ 0 and the rearranged inequality. The subcollection version is identical after replacing n and x_i by N and y_i.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary of the order-statistic inequality used to create many sums with the largest element
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let x_1 ≥ ... ≥ x_n and ∑X ≥ 0. Put j = floor(n/k). Then x_1 plus any k-1 elements from among the j largest elements of X\{x_1}, namely from {x_2,...,x_{j+1}}, has nonnegative sum. Consequently there are at least binom(floor(n/k),k-1) nonnegative k-sums involving x_1 of this form.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the order-statistic inequality with this j: x_1 ≥ -((n-j)/j)x_{j+1}. Since j ≤ n/k, one has (n-j)/j ≥ k-1. If x_{j+1} < 0, then -((n-j)/j)x_{j+1} ≥ -(k-1)x_{j+1}, so x_1 + (k-1)x_{j+1} ≥ 0; if x_{j+1} ≥ 0 the conclusion is immediate. Any k-1 chosen elements from {x_2,...,x_{j+1}} are each at least x_{j+1}, so their sum is at least (k-1)x_{j+1}. Thus each such choice together with x_1 is nonnegative.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2, quick exponential bound
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k and n be positive integers with n ≥ 3k^{k+1} + k^3. For every collection X = {x_1,...,x_n} of n real numbers with ∑X ≥ 0, the number of k-element subsets S satisfying ∑_{i in S} x_i ≥ 0 is at least binom(n-1,k-1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Order the numbers x_1 ≥ x_2 ≥ ... ≥ x_n. If x_1 plus the k-1 smallest elements is nonnegative, i.e. x_1 + ∑_{i=n-k+2}^n x_i ≥ 0, then every k-subset containing x_1 is nonnegative, because replacing the k-1 smallest elements by any other k-1 elements can only increase the sum. This already gives binom(n-1,k-1) nonnegative k-sums. Thus assume x_1 + ∑_{i=n-k+2}^n x_i < 0. Removing x_1 and these k-1 smallest elements leaves a collection with positive total sum. The paper then obtains many nonnegative k-sums avoiding these removed elements. If X has fewer than 2k negative elements, then at least n-2k elements are nonnegative, and all k-subsets of those elements are nonnegative; in the range of n under consideration this already gives more than binom(n-1,k-1). Otherwise, there are enough negative elements to delete at most k-1 further negative elements from the remaining collection so that its size m is a multiple of k and m > n-2k, while preserving nonnegative total. By the divisible case, this subcollection has at least binom(m-1,k-1) ≥ binom(n-2k,k-1) nonnegative k-sums, all avoiding x_1 and the k-1 smallest original elements. Independently, the order-statistic corollary gives at least binom(floor(n/k),k-1) nonnegative k-sums containing x_1. These two families are disjoint, so the total count is at least binom(n-2k,k-1) + binom(floor(n/k),k-1). The remaining task is to show this exceeds binom(n-1,k-1). Using elementary binomial estimates, it suffices to prove (n-3k)^{k-1} + (n/k-k)^{k-1} ≥ n^{k-1}. The paper bounds (a-b)^m below by the first term minus the second term in its binomial expansion when the expansion terms are decreasing. Under n > 3k(k-1), (n-3k)^{k-1} > n^{k-1} - (k-1)n^{k-2}3k. Under n > k^2(k-1), (n/k-k)^{k-1} > n^{k-1}/k^{k-1} - n^{k-2}/k^{k-4}. Adding these estimates reduces the desired inequality to n ≥ 3k^{k+1}+k^3, which is the theorem’s hypothesis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Central element definition and replacement-count estimate
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In a set Y of real numbers, an element y ∈ Y is called central if every k-sum in Y involving y is nonnegative. Suppose X is ordered x_1 ≥ ... ≥ x_n, and an iterative process has removed x_1,...,x_p together with p(k-1) very small elements, leaving a set X_{p+1} of size n-kp and nonnegative total whose largest element is x_{p+1}. If x_{p+1} is central in X_{p+1}, then the original collection X has at least (p+1)binom(n-kp-1,k-1) nonnegative k-sums.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since x_{p+1} is central in X_{p+1}, for every choice T of k-1 other elements of X_{p+1}, the sum x_{p+1} + ∑_{t in T} t is nonnegative. There are binom(n-kp-1,k-1) such choices T. For each such T, replace x_{p+1} by any one of x_1,...,x_p,x_{p+1}. Each replacement element is at least x_{p+1}, so the resulting k-sum in the original X remains nonnegative. For fixed T these p+1 k-subsets are distinct, and different T give different subsets, yielding (p+1)binom(n-kp-1,k-1) nonnegative k-sums.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Iteration comparison estimate in Theorem 3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the central-element iteration, the paper needs that for every integer p with 1 ≤ p < n/(2k), a central element appearing after p deletion steps would already imply more than binom(n-1,k-1) nonnegative k-sums. It is enough to prove the stronger numerical inequality (p+1)(n-k(p+1))^{k-1} > n^{k-1}, because binom(n-kp-1,k-1) is bounded below by roughly (n-k(p+1))^{k-1}/(k-1)! and binom(n-1,k-1) is bounded above by n^{k-1}/(k-1)!.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof splits p into two ranges. If p < n/k^2, the paper again uses the first-term-minus-second-term estimate: provided n > k^3, (n-k(p+1))^{k-1} > n^{k-1} - (p+1)k(k-1)n^{k-2}. Multiplying by p+1, the right-hand side exceeds n^{k-1} exactly when n/(k(k-1)) > p+2+1/p. Since p+2+1/p ≤ n/k^2 + 3 in this range, the condition follows from n > 3k^2(k-1). If n/k^2 ≤ p < n/(2k), then p+1 > n/k^2 and n-k(p+1) is bounded below in the paper by n/2 for the relevant range, giving (p+1)(n-k(p+1))^{k-1} > (n/k^2)(n/2)^{k-1} = n^k/(2^{k-1}k^2), which is greater than n^{k-1} when n > 2^{k-1}k^2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3, main improved MMS bound
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k and n be positive integers with n > k(4e log k)^k, where log is the natural logarithm. Then every collection X = {x_1,...,x_n} of n real numbers with ∑X ≥ 0 has at least binom(n-1,k-1) k-element subsets with nonnegative sum. Equivalently, f(k) ≤ k(4e log k)^k for the eventual threshold f(k) in the Manickam-Miklós-Singhi problem.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Order X as x_1 ≥ ... ≥ x_n and use the central-element notion. If x_1 is central in X, every k-subset containing x_1 is nonnegative, giving binom(n-1,k-1). Otherwise x_1 together with the k-1 smallest elements has negative sum; remove these k elements. The remaining collection X_2 has positive total. If x_2 is central in X_2, then the replacement-count argument gives 2binom(n-k-1,k-1) nonnegative k-sums in X; the paper checks this exceeds binom(n-1,k-1) for n > 2k^2. If x_2 is not central, remove x_2 and the k-1 smallest elements of X_2, leaving X_3 with positive total, and continue. In general, if a central element appears at stage p+1, the replacement-count estimate gives (p+1)binom(n-kp-1,k-1) nonnegative sums, and the iteration comparison estimate shows this is already enough for every p up to n/(2k). Thus one may assume that no central element appears during the first about n/(2k) stages. At that point the remaining set still has nonnegative total and size about n/2. Applying the order-statistic inequality inside this remaining set, the paper writes, for the largest remaining element x_{n/(2k)} and each 1 ≤ j ≤ n/2, the bound x_{n/(2k)} ≥ -((n/2-j)/j)x_{j+n/(2k)}. Taking j = n/(2 log k) gives x_{n/(2k)} > -(log k - 1)x_{n/(2k)+n/(2 log k)}. Hence any one element among the first n/(2k) original elements, together with log k - 1 elements from the next block of length n/(2 log k), has positive sum. Therefore any k-subset formed by taking k/log k elements from the first block and k-k/log k elements from the next block is nonnegative, by grouping the second-block elements into groups of size log k - 1 paired with each first-block element. The paper counts at least binom(n/(2k), k/log k) binom(n/(2 log k), k-k/log k) such k-sums, suppressing floor and ceiling notation. It then estimates binom(n/(2k), k/log k) ≥ n^{k/log k}/((4k)^{k/log k}(k/log k)!) and binom(n/(2 log k), k-k/log k) ≥ n^{k-k/log k}/((4 log k)^{k-k/log k}(k-k/log k)!). Multiplying and using 1/((k/log k)!(k-k/log k)!) ≥ 1/k!, k^{k/log k}=e^k, and (log k)^{k-k/log k} ≤ (log k)^k, this product is greater than n^k/(k!(4e log k)^k). This exceeds n^{k-1}/(k-1)!, and hence exceeds binom(n-1,k-1), exactly when n > k(4e log k)^k.

proof_techniques.name: Complementary-pair counting
proof_techniques.description: For unrestricted subsets, pair every subset with its complement. Since the two subset sums add to the total sum, nonnegativity of the total forces at least one member of each pair to be nonnegative. This gives a robust lower bound without needing any information about the distribution of the individual numbers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the Bernoulli problem at p = 1/2, the random subset is uniform over all subsets, and complement pairing of weights summing to 1 directly suggests a probability lower bound of 1/2 for exceeding threshold 1/2. Variants may also be useful for symmetry or self-complementary decompositions.

proof_techniques.name: Condition fixed-size layers and use MMS-type lower bounds
proof_techniques.description: The paper’s entire framework concerns uniform k-element subsets: for a deterministic vector with nonnegative total, it lower-bounds the number of k-subsets with nonnegative sum. The divisible case gives an exact lower bound using Baranyai partitions, while the main theorem gives the same lower bound once n is sufficiently large relative to k.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A Bernoulli subset can be conditioned on its size r, becoming a uniform r-subset. One can try applying these fixed-size results to centered numbers such as w_i minus a layer-dependent threshold, whenever the centered total is nonnegative on that layer.

proof_techniques.name: Baranyai parallel-class partition
proof_techniques.description: When k divides n, Baranyai’s theorem partitions all k-subsets of [n] into classes of n/k disjoint k-sets that cover [n]. In each class the sum of all block-sums equals the total sum of the underlying numbers, so at least one block has nonnegative sum. Counting the classes gives exactly binom(n-1,k-1) guaranteed nonnegative k-sums.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: After conditioning on a fixed subset size r, if the number of coordinates m is divisible by r, this gives a clean deterministic lower bound for the conditional fraction of good r-subsets. It may also guide approximation arguments by embedding into a nearby divisible ambient size.

proof_techniques.name: Order-statistic averaging inequality
proof_techniques.description: After sorting x_1 ≥ ... ≥ x_n, the paper repeatedly uses the fact that nonnegative total implies jx_1 + (n-j)x_{j+1} ≥ 0. This converts global average information into a pointwise comparison between the largest element and a later order statistic. It is then used to certify that the largest element plus many choices of moderately large elements forms a nonnegative k-sum.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For weighted Bernoulli questions, sorted weights or sorted centered weights may allow one to identify a large block of coordinates such that including one or more top weights compensates for many lower weights, yielding explicit families of above-threshold subsets inside a size layer.

proof_techniques.name: Delete a negative obstruction while preserving nonnegative total
proof_techniques.description: If the largest element is not central, then it forms a negative k-sum with the k-1 smallest elements. Removing exactly those k elements removes a negative total contribution, so the remaining collection still has nonnegative, indeed positive, total sum. This enables an iterative argument or lets one trim further negative elements to reach a size divisible by k.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For centered layer variables, a bad certificate can sometimes be removed while keeping the centered total nonnegative. This suggests induction or decomposition over troublesome coordinates rather than trying to handle all weights at once.

proof_techniques.name: Central element and replacement amplification
proof_techniques.description: An element is central if every k-sum containing it is nonnegative. If a central element appears in a remaining set after some larger elements have been deleted, every central k-sum can be amplified: replacing the central element by any previously deleted larger element preserves nonnegativity. Thus one central element at a later stage can generate multiple disjoint families of nonnegative k-sums.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In a fixed-size layer of the Bernoulli problem, if a coordinate is sufficiently large that all completions are good after centering, then larger coordinates also work. This can multiply a conditional lower bound across several high-weight coordinates.

proof_techniques.name: Iterative largest-plus-smallest deletion dichotomy
proof_techniques.description: The main proof repeatedly checks whether the current largest remaining element is central. If yes, replacement amplification gives enough nonnegative k-sums; if not, remove that largest element together with the k-1 smallest remaining elements, preserving nonnegative total. After many noncentral steps, the structure forced by the repeated failures is strong enough to build many nonnegative sums from two order-statistic blocks.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This dichotomy could be ported to conditioned Bernoulli layers: either a high weight gives many successful subsets, or repeated failures imply inequalities among order statistics that may themselves create many successful subsets.

proof_techniques.name: Two-block construction after no central element appears
proof_techniques.description: If the central-element stopping condition never occurs during the first n/(2k) stages, the paper applies the order-statistic inequality in the remaining half-sized set to show that one element from a first high block plus log k - 1 elements from a following middle block has positive sum. Grouping then shows that any subset with k/log k elements from the high block and k-k/log k from the middle block is nonnegative. A product of binomial coefficients counts these subsets.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For weighted threshold events, a similar block construction might produce many above-threshold fixed-size subsets by mixing a controlled number of top weights with many medium weights, especially when direct star-type counting is insufficient.

proof_techniques.name: First-term-minus-second-term binomial expansion estimates
proof_techniques.description: To compare counts such as binom(n-2k,k-1)+binom(floor(n/k),k-1) with binom(n-1,k-1), the paper reduces to polynomial inequalities like (n-a)^{k-1} + (n/b-c)^{k-1} ≥ n^{k-1}. It then uses the elementary fact that if the terms in a binomial expansion are decreasing from the start, (p-q)^m can be bounded below by the first term minus the second term.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Any fixed-size-layer proof for the Bernoulli problem will require summing or comparing binomial counts. These coarse but explicit estimates are useful for turning combinatorial constructions into clean parameter ranges.

other_useful_info: Definitions and context from the paper: A(n,k) is the minimum possible number of nonnegative k-sums among n real numbers with nonnegative total. The Manickam-Miklós-Singhi conjecture states that A(n,k)=binom(n-1,k-1) for all n ≥ 4k. Known results listed in the paper include: the divisible case k|n; the conjecture for k=2 and k=3; existence of an eventual threshold f(k); and the Manickam-Miklós propagation theorem that A(n,k)=binom(n-1,k-1) implies A(n+k,k)=binom(n+k-1,k-1) and A(cn,k)=binom(cn-1,k-1) for all c. The paper gives a counterexample pattern for n=3k+1 and k>2: take 3 repeated 3k-2 times and -3k+2 repeated 3 times; then total sum is 0, any k-subset containing a negative element has negative sum, and the number of nonnegative k-sums is binom(n-3,k), which is less than binom(n-1,k-1). Theorem 3’s displayed statement in the text appears to have a variable typo, but the abstract, proof, and equivalent f(k) formulation make clear that the intended condition is n > k(4e log k)^k.
why_relevant: The Manickam–Miklós–Singhi line of work is the fixed-size-subset analogue of asking when random subsets have above-average weight. Conditioning the Bernoulli subset on its size suggests these combinatorial nonnegative-sum tools as a possible route.

title: Tight Lower Bound on the Probability of a Binomial Exceeding its Expectation
overall_summary: The paper proves a sharp universal lower bound for an upper tail at the mean of a binomial random variable: if X is distributed as B(m,p) and p > 1/m, then P[X ≥ E[X]] > 1/4. The proof first shows that, between the discontinuities caused by ceil(mp), the function p ↦ P[B(m,p) ≥ mp] is increasing, so the problem reduces to bounding critical binomial CDFs P[B(m,k/m) ≤ k]. The critical case k = 1 is handled exactly by calculus, while all k ≥ 2 are handled using the Camp-Paulson nonlinear normal approximation to the binomial CDF and a sequence of uniform parameter bounds. For the original weighted Bernoulli problem, the main direct value is in the equal-weight subcase, where the event becomes a binomial tail at or just above its mean; the monotonicity reduction and critical-parameter analysis may also suggest finite reductions or extremal tests.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1, main result
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any positive integer m and any probability p with p > 1/m, let X be a binomial random variable with m trials and success probability p, denoted X ~ B(m,p). Then P[X ≥ E[X]] > 1/4, where E[X] = mp.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write F(m,p) = P[X ≥ E[X]] = sum_{j=ceil(mp)}^m binom(m,j) p^j (1-p)^(m-j). The case p = 1 is immediate, and m = 1 is irrelevant because no probability p satisfies p > 1. For p in (1/m,1), Corollary 1 reduces the desired lower bound to proving max_{1≤k≤m-1} P_{Y~B(m,k/m)}[Y ≤ k] ≤ 3/4. The proof then splits the maximum into k = 1 and k ≥ 2. Lemma 4 proves P_{B(m,1/m)}[X ≤ 1] ≤ 3/4, with equality at m = 2. Corollary 2 proves the stronger bound P_{B(m,k/m)}[X ≤ k] ≤ 0.7152 for all k ≥ 2 in the critical range. Hence F(m,p) ≥ 1 - max{3/4,0.7152} = 1/4. The theorem states the strict inequality; the paper also notes that the lower bound is never attained and is approached asymptotically for m = 2 as p tends to 1/2 from the right. The strictness is consistent with the strict piecewise monotonicity in Lemma 1: the only 3/4 critical CDF produces only a limiting left-endpoint value for the upper-tail probability, not an attained value under p > 1/m.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1, piecewise monotonicity reduction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let m be a positive integer. For every integer k = 1,2,...,m-1 and every p in the half-open interval (k/m,(k+1)/m], if X ~ B(m,p), then P[X ≥ E[X]] ≥ P[Y ≥ k+1], where Y ~ B(m,k/m) and E[X] = mp.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define F(m,p) = P_{X~B(m,p)}[X ≥ E[X]]. Since X is integer-valued, F(m,p) = sum_{j=ceil(mp)}^m binom(m,j) p^j (1-p)^(m-j). On an interval I_r = ((r-1)/m,r/m], the value ceil(mp) is fixed and equal to r, so F(m,p) = sum_{j=r}^m binom(m,j) p^j (1-p)^(m-j). Differentiating on the interior of I_r gives ∂F/∂p = sum_{j=r}^m binom(m,j) (1-p)^(m-j-1) p^(j-1) (j - mp). For p in I_r one has r ≥ mp, and each summation index satisfies j ≥ r, so each factor j - mp is nonnegative; away from endpoints the derivative is in fact positive because at least one term is positive. Thus p ↦ F(m,p) is increasing on each interval I_r. Taking r = k+1, for p in (k/m,(k+1)/m] the smallest value is the right limit as p decreases to k/m, namely sum_{j=k+1}^m binom(m,j) (k/m)^j (1-k/m)^(m-j) = P_{Y~B(m,k/m)}[Y ≥ k+1].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1, reduction to finitely many critical CDFs
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every positive integer m and every p in (1/m,1), if X ~ B(m,p), then P[X ≥ E[X]] ≥ 1 - max_{k in {1,...,m-1}} P[Y_k ≤ k], where Y_k ~ B(m,k/m).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a given p in (1/m,1), Lemma 1 places p in one of the intervals (k/m,(k+1)/m] and gives P_{B(m,p)}[X ≥ E[X]] ≥ P_{B(m,k/m)}[Y_k ≥ k+1]. Taking the minimum over all possible k = 1,...,m-1 gives a uniform lower bound. Since P[Y_k ≥ k+1] = 1 - P[Y_k ≤ k], the minimum over upper tails is equal to 1 minus the maximum of the corresponding lower-tail CDFs.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Camp-Paulson normal approximation, quoted estimate used in Lemma 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper quotes the following Camp-Paulson approximation to the binomial CDF. If X ~ B(m,p), Φ is the standard normal distribution function, and the parameters a, b, r, c, μ, σ are defined by a = 1/(9m - 9j), b = 1/(9j + 9), r = ((j+1)(1-p))/(mp - jp), c = (1-b) r^(1/3), μ = 1-a, and σ = sqrt(b r^(2/3) + a), then, in the parameter regime where these quantities are defined, |P[X ≤ j] - Φ((c-μ)/σ)| ≤ 0.007/sqrt(mp(1-p)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not prove this estimate; it cites Camp-Paulson's normal approximation as recorded in references [6,5,7]. Its role in the proof is to convert the critical CDF P_{B(m,k/m)}[X ≤ k] into a standard normal CDF with explicitly computable argument plus an error term. Substituting p = k/m and j = k simplifies the error to 0.007/sqrt(k(1-k/m)) and yields an argument expressible in terms of the auxiliary quantities α_k, β_k, and γ_{m,k} used in Lemma 2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2, Camp-Paulson bound for critical binomial CDFs
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every integer m ≥ 2 and every k = 1,2,...,m-1, let X ~ B(m,k/m). Let Φ be the standard normal distribution function, define β_k = (1/(1+k))·(1+1/k)^(2/3), γ_{m,k} = 1/(m-k), and θ = 17/(3·2^(1/3)) - 3·2^(1/3), approximately 0.71787. Then P[X ≤ k] ≤ Φ((β_k θ + (1/3)γ_{m,k})/sqrt(β_k + γ_{m,k})) + 0.007/sqrt(1 - 1/m).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the quoted Camp-Paulson bound with p = k/m and j = k. Direct substitution and simplification give P[X ≤ k] ≤ Φ((α_k + (1/3)γ_{m,k})/sqrt(β_k + γ_{m,k})) + 0.007/sqrt(k(1-k/m)), where α_k = (1+1/k)^(1/3)·(3 - 1/(3(1+k))) - 3, β_k = (1/(1+k))(1+1/k)^(2/3), and γ_{m,k} = 1/(m-k). The proof then uniformly bounds α_k by θβ_k. To do this, set λ = (1+1/k)^(1/3), so λ lies in (1,2^(1/3)]. Algebra gives α_k/β_k = 3λ/(1+λ+λ^2) - 1/(3λ). Differentiating shows this ratio is increasing on (1,2^(1/3)]; the paper verifies positivity by rewriting the derivative with numerator 9 - 30(λ-1)^2 - 30(λ-1)^3 - 8(λ-1)^4 and using λ-1 ≤ 2^(1/3)-1 < 0.26, which makes the subtracted part less than 9. Therefore α_k/β_k is at most its value at λ = 2^(1/3), which is θ. Since Φ is increasing, α_k can be replaced by θβ_k in the normal argument. Finally, the error term is bounded uniformly: for k = 1,...,m-1, the quantity k(1-k/m) = k(m-k)/m is minimized at the endpoints and is at least 1 - 1/m. Hence 0.007/sqrt(k(1-k/m)) ≤ 0.007/sqrt(1 - 1/m), proving the lemma.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3, removal of γ-dependence in the normal argument
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let m > 1 and k = 1,2,...,m-1. Define β_k = (1/(1+k))(1+1/k)^(2/3), γ_{m,k} = 1/(m-k), and θ = 17/(3·2^(1/3)) - 3·2^(1/3). Then Φ((β_k θ + (1/3)γ_{m,k})/sqrt(β_k + γ_{m,k})) ≤ Φ((β_k θ + 1/3)/sqrt(β_k + 1)), where Φ is the standard normal distribution function.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because γ_{m,k} = 1/(m-k) lies in [1/(m-1),1], it is enough to maximize φ(γ) = (β_k θ + γ/3)/sqrt(β_k + γ) over γ in [0,1]. The derivative is φ'(γ) = (γ + β_k(2 - 3θ))/(6(β_k+γ)^(3/2)). Since 2 - 3θ is negative, φ decreases until γ = β_k(3θ-2) and increases afterward; hence its maximum on [0,1] occurs at an endpoint. The endpoint values are φ(0) = θ sqrt(β_k) and φ(1) = (β_k θ + 1/3)/sqrt(β_k+1). The proof checks that φ(0) ≤ φ(1) whenever β_k ≤ 1/(θ(9θ-6)), which is approximately 3.022. Since β_k is decreasing in k and β_1 = 2^(-1/3) ≈ 0.7937, this condition always holds. Thus the maximum is attained at γ = 1, and applying the monotonicity of Φ gives the stated inequality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2, uniform bound for critical CDFs with k ≥ 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For integers m ≥ 2 and k with 2 ≤ k ≤ m-1, if X ~ B(m,k/m), then P[X ≤ k] ≤ 0.7152.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Combine Lemmas 2 and 3 to get P[X ≤ k] ≤ Φ(g(β_k)) + 0.007/sqrt(1 - 1/m), where g(β) = (βθ + 1/3)/sqrt(β+1). For k ≥ 2, β_k lies in [0,β_2], with β_2 = 1/(2^(2/3)3^(1/3)) ≈ 0.43679, because β_k decreases with k. The derivative g'(β) = (3(β+2)θ - 1)/(6(β+1)^(3/2)) is positive on this interval, since already 6θ - 1 > 0, so g is maximized at β_2. The paper computes g(β_2) < 0.53968 and hence Φ(g(β_2)) < 0.7053. The error term 0.007/sqrt(1 - 1/m) is decreasing in m and is at most its value at m = 2, namely less than 0.0099. Therefore P[X ≤ k] ≤ 0.7053 + 0.0099 = 0.7152.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4, exact bound for the critical case k = 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every integer m ≥ 2, let X ~ B(m,1/m). Then P[X ≤ 1] = (1 - 1/m)^m + (1 - 1/m)^(m-1) ≤ 3/4. Equality occurs at m = 2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The exact expression follows from the j = 0 and j = 1 binomial terms: P[X ≤ 1] = (1 - 1/m)^m + m(1/m)(1 - 1/m)^(m-1) = (1 - 1/m)^m + (1 - 1/m)^(m-1). Denote this expression by ρ(m), extending it to real m ≥ 2. Direct differentiation gives ρ'(m) = (m-1)^(m-1)m^(-m)·(2 + (2m-1) log(1 - 1/m)). Thus ρ'(m) ≤ 0 is equivalent to 2 + (2m-1)log(1 - 1/m) ≤ 0. Using the expansion -log(1 - 1/m) = sum_{r≥1} 1/(r m^r) and retaining the first three positive terms gives -(2m-1)log(1 - 1/m) ≥ (2m-1)(1/m + 1/(2m^2) + 1/(3m^3)) = 2 + 1/(6m^2) - 1/(3m^3) ≥ 2 for m ≥ 2. Hence ρ is nonincreasing on [2,∞). Since ρ(2) = (1/2)^2 + (1/2) = 3/4, the result follows for all integer m ≥ 2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3, lower-tail analogue
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any positive integer m and any probability p satisfying p < 1 - 1/m, let X ~ B(m,p). Then P[X ≤ E[X]] > 1/4, where E[X] = mp.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let G(m,p) = P[X ≤ E[X]] = sum_{j=0}^{floor(mp)} binom(m,j) p^j(1-p)^(m-j), and let F(m,q) = P_{Z~B(m,q)}[Z ≥ E[Z]] = sum_{t=ceil(mq)}^m binom(m,t) q^t(1-q)^(m-t). Put q = 1-p. Changing variables t = m-j in G(m,p) gives G(m,p) = sum_{t=m-floor(m(1-q))}^m binom(m,m-t)(1-q)^(m-t)q^t. Since m - floor(m(1-q)) = ceil(mq) and binom(m,m-t) = binom(m,t), this is exactly F(m,q). The hypothesis p < 1 - 1/m is equivalent to q > 1/m, so Theorem 1 applies to F(m,q) and yields G(m,p) > 1/4.

proof_techniques.name: Piecewise monotonicity of the binomial upper tail
proof_techniques.description: The function F(m,p) = P_{B(m,p)}[X ≥ mp] has jumps only when ceil(mp) changes. On each interval ((r-1)/m,r/m], the lower summation index is fixed at r, and differentiating the binomial tail gives a sum whose terms are proportional to j - mp. Since all indices j in the tail satisfy j ≥ r ≥ mp, the derivative is nonnegative, and in the interior it is strictly positive. This turns the infimum over an interval into a left-endpoint limit.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For equal weights w_i = 1/m in the original problem, the relevant probability is exactly this F(m,p). This technique can reduce equal-weight checks over a continuum of p values to the lattice thresholds p = k/m; for unequal weights, an analogous interval decomposition would require tracking which subsets cross the weighted threshold, but the clean sign argument is special to the binomial/equal-weight case.

proof_techniques.name: Reduction to critical CDFs at p = k/m
proof_techniques.description: After the monotonicity step, the lower bound on P[B(m,p) ≥ mp] is controlled by critical left limits P_{B(m,k/m)}[X ≥ k+1]. Complementing these tails reduces the proof to a uniform upper bound on P_{B(m,k/m)}[X ≤ k] for k = 1,...,m-1. This is useful because the mean of B(m,k/m) is exactly k, so all critical distributions are indexed by integer means.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In equal-weight tests for the original problem, the worst values of p for a fixed m should be sought near threshold crossings k/m. This finite critical family can provide necessary examples, sanity checks, or base cases for more general weighted arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Camp-Paulson nonlinear normal approximation
proof_techniques.description: For the bulk critical cases k ≥ 2, the paper avoids crude Chernoff-style estimates and uses the Camp-Paulson approximation, which transforms the binomial CDF into a standard normal CDF with an explicit error 0.007/sqrt(mp(1-p)). The transformation contains cube roots and correction terms designed to capture binomial skewness better than the classical normal approximation. After substitution p = k/m and j = k, the normal argument is expressed in parameters β_k and γ_{m,k}, enabling uniform maximization.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Whenever a solver needs sharp numerical control of equal-weight binomial tails near the mean, this is a citable route to constants. It may also help analyze blocks of equal weights or compare a weighted Bernoulli sum to a binomial benchmark.

proof_techniques.name: Uniform parameter domination after approximation
proof_techniques.description: The paper does not merely quote a normal approximation; it simplifies the resulting expression by proving α_k ≤ θβ_k and then bounding the remaining dependence on γ_{m,k} by its endpoint γ = 1. This converts a two-parameter family of CDF bounds into a one-variable maximization over β, and then into an explicit numerical constant. The key moves are monotonicity of α_k/β_k after the substitution λ = (1+1/k)^(1/3), endpoint maximization of a unimodal function in γ, and monotonicity of g(β) over the relevant β-range.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This style of argument can be reused if an original-problem reduction leads to a parametric family of binomial or Poisson-binomial estimates: introduce normalized parameters, prove monotonicity or endpoint domination, and isolate a small exceptional case.

proof_techniques.name: Exact treatment of the small-mean exceptional case
proof_techniques.description: The normal-approximation argument is not used for k = 1. Instead, the paper computes P_{B(m,1/m)}[X ≤ 1] exactly as (1 - 1/m)^m + (1 - 1/m)^(m-1) and proves this expression decreases for m ≥ 2 by differentiating its continuous extension. The maximum is therefore the m = 2 value 3/4, which is exactly the extremal value needed for the global theorem.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Extremizers in weighted Bernoulli problems often occur in low-dimensional or small-effective-mean regimes. This suggests isolating such regimes for exact computation rather than relying only on asymptotic or concentration estimates.

proof_techniques.name: Complementation symmetry for lower tails
proof_techniques.description: The paper derives a lower-tail analogue by replacing p with q = 1-p and changing variables t = m-j in the binomial sum. This identifies P_{B(m,p)}[X ≤ mp] with P_{B(m,1-p)}[X ≥ m(1-p)] up to the floor/ceiling conversion, allowing Theorem 1 to be applied directly.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original weighted Bernoulli problem, considering complements 1 - v_i changes the weighted sum to 1 - sum_i w_i v_i. This may let high-p or lower-tail formulations be converted into upper-tail formulations, at least when the threshold structure is compatible.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly states that the 1/4 lower bound is tight but never attained; it is approached asymptotically in the case m = 2 as p tends to 1/2 from the right. It notes that p = 1 is trivial and that m = 1 is irrelevant under the hypothesis p > 1/m. The authors mention that Rigollet and Tong (2011) contain a proof of a similar theorem only for bias p < 1/2, while their theorem covers the full range p > 1/m. The Camp-Paulson approximation used in the proof is cited to Johnson, Kotz, and Balakrishnan; Johnson, Kemp, and Kotz; and Lesch and Jeske.
why_relevant: The equal-weight case reduces exactly to binomial tails at or just above the mean. This paper gives sharp lower bounds and extremal behavior for such binomial tail probabilities.

title: On Some Conjectures of Samuels and Feige
overall_summary: The paper studies independent nonnegative random variables X_1,...,X_n with E X_i = 1 and a normalized weighted sum Z = ∑ μ_i X_i, where μ_1 ≥ ... ≥ μ_n > 0 and ∑ μ_i = 1. Its main result is conditional: Samuels' product-form conjectural lower bound on P(Z < 1+δ) implies Feige's simpler conjectural lower bound P(Z < 1+δ) ≥ min(δ/(δ+M), 1/e), where M is the largest weight. The proof introduces a logarithmic transform Φ of the product factors, uses monotonicity of Φ in the normalized weight, and then uses concavity of Φ along rays to reduce a continuous minimization to endpoint cases. For the original Bernoulli-weighted-sum problem, the most transferable ingredients are the log-product normalization, the reduction of weight-vector dependence to largest-weight and endpoint configurations, and the sharp two-point extremal examples, since scaled Bernoulli variables are also nonnegative two-point mean-one variables.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n be a positive integer, let μ_1 ≥ ... ≥ μ_n > 0 with μ_1 + ... + μ_n = 1, let X_1,...,X_n be independent nonnegative random variables with E X_i = 1 for every i, and set Z = ∑_{i=1}^n μ_i X_i. Let M = μ_1, let δ > 0, and let T = 1 + δ. The theorem states that the universal validity of Samuels' product lower bound in this setting implies the universal validity of Feige's lower bound in this setting. Explicitly, if for all such data one has P(Z < T) ≥ min_{1≤i≤n} ∏_{j=1}^i (1 − μ_j/(T − ∑_{k=i+1}^n μ_k)), then for all such data one has P(Z < T) ≥ min(δ/(δ+M), 1/e).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume the Samuels product bound. Define partial sums σ_i = ∑_{k=1}^i μ_k. Since T − ∑_{k=i+1}^n μ_k = 1+δ − (1−σ_i) = σ_i + δ, Samuels gives P(Z < 1+δ) ≥ min_i ∏_{j=1}^i (1 − μ_j/(σ_i+δ)). Choose an index i attaining the minimum. Introduce Φ on [0,1] × (0,∞) by Φ(μ,ρ) = μ^{-1} log(1 − μ/(1+ρ)) for μ > 0 and Φ(0,ρ) = −1/(1+ρ). For j ≤ i, the identity Φ(μ_j/σ_i, δ/σ_i) = (σ_i/μ_j) log(1 − μ_j/(σ_i+δ)) rewrites the logarithm of the product as ∑_{j=1}^i (μ_j/σ_i) Φ(μ_j/σ_i, δ/σ_i). The coefficients μ_j/σ_i sum to 1. Since μ_j ≤ M and Lemma 3.2 says Φ is decreasing in its first variable, each term Φ(μ_j/σ_i, δ/σ_i) is at least Φ(M/σ_i, δ/σ_i), hence log P(Z < 1+δ) ≥ Φ(M/σ_i, δ/σ_i). Now M ≤ σ_i ≤ 1, so the right side is bounded below by min_{σ∈[M,1]} Φ(M/σ, δ/σ). Put t = M/σ; then t ranges over [M,1] and the expression becomes Φ(t, (δ/M)t). Lemma 3.4 says t ↦ Φ(t, (δ/M)t) is concave, so its minimum on [M,1] occurs at an endpoint. Therefore log P(Z < 1+δ) ≥ min(Φ(1, δ/M), Φ(M,δ)) = min(log(δ/(δ+M)), (1/M) log(1 − M/(1+δ))). Finally Lemma 3.3, applied with μ = M and ρ = δ, gives exp(Φ(M,δ)) ≥ min(δ/(δ+M), 1/e). Combining this with the exact value of Φ(1,δ/M) yields P(Z < 1+δ) ≥ min(δ/(δ+M), 1/e).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every real t in the interval [0,1], one has 1 − t/(1 + t/(e−1)) ≥ e^{−t}, where e is the base of the natural logarithm.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The desired inequality is rearranged as 1 − e^{−t} ≥ t/(1+t/(e−1)), or equivalently f(t) ≥ 0 for f(t) = (1 + t/(e−1))(1 − e^{−t}) − t. The paper computes f(0)=0, f'(t) = (1/(e−1)) e^{−t}((e−2)(1−e^t)+t), and f''(t) = (1/(e−1)) e^{−t}(3−e−t). Thus f is convex on [0,3−e] and concave on [3−e,1]. Since f'(0)=0, convexity gives that f is increasing on [0,3−e], so f(3−e) ≥ f(0)=0. On [3−e,1], concavity and the endpoint facts f(3−e) ≥ 0 and f(1)=0 imply f(t) ≥ 0 throughout that interval. Hence f(t) ≥ 0 for all t∈[0,1].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define Φ: [0,1] × (0,∞) → R by Φ(μ,ρ) = μ^{-1} log(1 − μ/(1+ρ)) when μ∈(0,1], and Φ(0,ρ) = −1/(1+ρ). If ρ > 0 and λ, μ ∈ [0,1] satisfy λ < μ, then Φ(μ,ρ) < Φ(λ,ρ). In other words, for fixed positive ρ, Φ is strictly decreasing in its first argument.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define φ on [0,1) by φ(s) = −∑_{k≥0} s^k/(k+1), equivalently φ(s)=log(1−s)/s for s∈(0,1) and φ(0)=−1. The power series has strictly negative coefficients in the sense that as s increases each nonconstant term makes the value smaller, so φ is strictly decreasing on [0,1). For μ∈[0,1], one can write Φ(μ,ρ) = (1/(1+ρ)) φ(μ/(1+ρ)), with the μ=0 case matching by definition. Since ρ>0, the argument μ/(1+ρ) lies in [0,1), and λ<μ implies λ/(1+ρ)<μ/(1+ρ). The strict decrease of φ gives Φ(μ,ρ)<Φ(λ,ρ).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Product-to-Φ averaging estimate used in the proof of Theorem 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let a_1,...,a_i be positive real numbers, let σ = a_1+...+a_i, let M = max_{1≤j≤i} a_j, and let δ>0. With Φ defined by Φ(u,r)=u^{-1} log(1−u/(1+r)) for u>0 and Φ(0,r)=−1/(1+r), one has log ∏_{j=1}^i (1 − a_j/(σ+δ)) = ∑_{j=1}^i (a_j/σ) Φ(a_j/σ, δ/σ) ≥ Φ(M/σ, δ/σ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equality is obtained by substituting u=a_j/σ and r=δ/σ into the definition of Φ: multiplying Φ(a_j/σ,δ/σ) by a_j/σ gives log(1−a_j/(σ+δ)). The numbers a_j/σ are nonnegative and sum to 1. Since a_j/σ ≤ M/σ for every j, Lemma 3.2 gives Φ(a_j/σ,δ/σ) ≥ Φ(M/σ,δ/σ). Therefore their weighted average is at least Φ(M/σ,δ/σ). In the paper this is applied with a_j=μ_j for j≤i and σ=σ_i.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With Φ: [0,1] × (0,∞) → R defined by Φ(μ,ρ)=μ^{-1} log(1−μ/(1+ρ)) for μ>0 and Φ(0,ρ)=−1/(1+ρ), for every μ∈[0,1] and every ρ>0 one has Φ(μ,ρ) ≥ log(min(ρ/(μ+ρ), 1/e)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The endpoint μ=0 is immediate because Φ(0,ρ)=−1/(1+ρ)>−1=log(1/e), while min(ρ/(0+ρ),1/e)=1/e. For μ=1, Φ(1,ρ)=log(ρ/(1+ρ))=log(ρ/(μ+ρ)), which is at least the logarithm of the stated minimum. Now fix μ∈(0,1). The paper notes that Φ is smooth and that its partial derivative in ρ is ∂_2 Φ(μ,ρ)=1/((1+ρ)(1+ρ−μ))>0, so Φ increases with ρ. First suppose ρ ≥ μ/(e−1). Then ρ/(μ+ρ) ≥ 1/e, so it suffices to show Φ(μ,ρ) ≥ −1. By monotonicity in ρ, Φ(μ,ρ) ≥ Φ(μ, μ/(e−1)); Lemma 3.1 with t=μ gives 1 − μ/(1+μ/(e−1)) ≥ e^{−μ}, hence Φ(μ, μ/(e−1)) ≥ −1. Second suppose 0<ρ≤μ/(e−1). Then ρ/(μ+ρ)≤1/e, so the desired bound is Φ(μ,ρ) ≥ log(ρ/(μ+ρ)). Let g(ρ)=Φ(μ,ρ)−log(ρ/(μ+ρ)). The derivative computed in the paper is g'(ρ)= −(1−μ)((1+ρ)μ−ρ^2)/(ρ(1+ρ)(1+ρ−μ)(μ+ρ)). Since ρ≤μ/(e−1)<μ, the factor ((1+ρ)μ−ρ^2) is positive, so g'(ρ)<0 on this interval. Thus g(ρ) ≥ g(μ/(e−1)). At ρ=μ/(e−1), the ratio ρ/(μ+ρ) equals 1/e, so g(μ/(e−1))=Φ(μ, μ/(e−1))+1≥0 by the first case. This proves the bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let α>0. With Φ defined by Φ(μ,ρ)=μ^{-1} log(1−μ/(1+ρ)) for μ>0 and Φ(0,ρ)=−1/(1+ρ), the function h_α given on (0,1] by h_α(t)=Φ(t,αt) and extended continuously to h_α(0)=−1 is concave on [0,1].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For t∈(0,1), h_α(t)=t^{-1} log(1 − t/(1+αt)). It is enough to show h_α''(t)≤0 on (0,1), since the endpoint values are handled by continuity. The paper defines η(t)=t^3 h_α''(t) and computes η(t)= t(2+(6α−3)t+4(α^2−α)t^2)/((1−t+αt)^2(1+αt)^2) + 2 log(1 − t/(1+αt)). Differentiating gives η'(t)= − t^2(1 + 3(1−2α+2(α−α^2)t)^2)/(2(1−t+αt)^3(1+αt)^3). For α>0 and t∈(0,1), the denominator is positive and the numerator is positive, so η'(t)<0. Since lim_{s→0} η(s)=0, η(t)<0 for all t∈(0,1). Therefore h_α''(t)=η(t)/t^3<0 on (0,1), proving concavity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Endpoint minimization consequence of Lemma 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M∈(0,1] and δ>0. With Φ as above, the paper uses the identity min_{σ∈[M,1]} Φ(M/σ, δ/σ) = min_{t∈[M,1]} Φ(t, (δ/M)t) = min(Φ(1,δ/M), Φ(M,δ)). Equivalently, this minimum is min(log(δ/(δ+M)), (1/M) log(1 − M/(1+δ))).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Substitute t=M/σ. As σ runs over [M,1], t runs over [M,1] with the order reversed, and δ/σ=(δ/M)t. Hence the minimization becomes that of h_{δ/M}(t)=Φ(t,(δ/M)t) over t∈[M,1]. By Lemma 3.4, h_{δ/M} is concave on [0,1], hence also on [M,1]. A concave function on a compact interval is bounded below by the smaller of its endpoint values, so the minimum over [M,1] is attained at t=M or t=1. Evaluating Φ at those endpoints gives Φ(1,δ/M)=log(δ/(δ+M)) and Φ(M,δ)=(1/M)log(1−M/(1+δ)).

proof_techniques.name: Logarithmic normalization of product bounds
proof_techniques.description: The proof converts Samuels' product ∏_{j=1}^i (1 − μ_j/(σ_i+δ)) into a sum of logarithms and then into a weighted average of values of the auxiliary function Φ. The normalization by σ_i is essential: the factors become Φ(μ_j/σ_i, δ/σ_i), and the coefficients μ_j/σ_i form a probability vector. This turns a multiplicative expression depending on many weights into an averaged analytic expression on normalized weights.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For weighted Bernoulli sums, probabilities of events such as all variables in a selected set failing or succeeding naturally produce products over weights or over Bernoulli probabilities. A similar log-and-normalize step could help reduce a complicated product or generating-function coefficient estimate to an inequality involving normalized weights.

proof_techniques.name: Collapse to the largest normalized weight via monotonicity
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.2 proves that Φ is strictly decreasing in its first argument. Once the logarithm of the product is written as ∑ (μ_j/σ_i) Φ(μ_j/σ_i,δ/σ_i), every term is bounded below by replacing μ_j/σ_i with the largest possible normalized weight M/σ_i. This removes all detailed dependence on μ_1,...,μ_i except through M and σ_i.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A proof of the original inequality may need to hold uniformly over all weight vectors. Establishing monotonicity of an appropriate transform with respect to individual weights could similarly reduce the search for worst cases to configurations governed by the maximum weight.

proof_techniques.name: Concavity along rays and endpoint reduction
proof_techniques.description: After the largest-weight reduction, the remaining unknown is the partial sum σ_i. The substitution t=M/σ_i makes the two arguments of Φ proportional: Φ(M/σ_i,δ/σ_i)=Φ(t,(δ/M)t). Lemma 3.4 proves concavity of this one-variable function, so the minimum over the whole interval is attained at endpoints. The endpoints correspond to σ_i=M and σ_i=1, i.e. the two extreme regimes recovered in Feige's bound.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests looking for a one-parameter concavity principle in the Bernoulli problem that reduces arbitrary weight distributions to endpoint configurations, such as one dominant weight versus a fully spread or fully accumulated set of weights.

proof_techniques.name: Regime split at the 1/e crossing
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.3 compares Φ(μ,ρ) to log(min(ρ/(μ+ρ),1/e)) by splitting at ρ=μ/(e−1), exactly where ρ/(μ+ρ)=1/e. Above the crossing, monotonicity of Φ in ρ and the scalar inequality of Lemma 3.1 give the universal −1 lower bound. Below the crossing, the derivative of g(ρ)=Φ(μ,ρ)−log(ρ/(μ+ρ)) is negative, so the endpoint value at the crossing controls the interval.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original problem leads to a comparison between a weight-dependent obstruction and a universal constant or threshold in p, this two-regime method gives a template: identify the crossing point, prove a universal bound on one side, and use derivative monotonicity to propagate it on the other.

proof_techniques.name: Two-point extremizers for mean-one nonnegative variables
proof_techniques.description: The paper records equality examples for the conjectured bounds in which each random variable is either 0 or a single compensating high value, sometimes with other variables fixed at 1. For Samuels' product, fixing variables after index i at 1 and making variables up to i two-point produces equality because Z<T occurs exactly when all first i variables take value 0. These examples show that product probabilities arise naturally from zero-or-large-value extremizers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: After scaling by p, Bernoulli(p) variables become two-point nonnegative mean-one variables taking 0 and 1/p. Thus the extremal shapes emphasized in the paper are structurally close to the variables in the original problem, even though the paper's threshold is 1+δ rather than the boundary threshold 1.

proof_techniques.name: Sharpness analysis through largest-weight and many-small-weights examples
proof_techniques.description: Feige's conjectured lower bound has two different sharpness mechanisms in the introduction: a single largest random coordinate realizes the δ/(δ+M) term, while equal small weights with rare large values show that the universal constant cannot exceed 1/e. The proof of Theorem 1.1 mirrors this dichotomy through endpoint minimization.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the Bernoulli problem, this points to testing and possibly proving against a small set of extremal weight patterns, especially one large coordinate and equal-weight configurations. Such tests can help identify the correct range of p before pursuing a general-weight argument.

other_useful_info: The paper states but does not prove Samuels' conjecture; its main contribution is the implication from Samuels' conjecture to Feige's conjecture. The introduction records sharpness examples. For Feige's δ/(δ+M) term, if M=μ_1, X_2=...=X_n=1, and X_1 equals (δ+M)/M with probability M/(δ+M) and 0 with probability δ/(δ+M), then P(Z<T)=δ/(δ+M). For the universal constant, if μ_1=...=μ_n=1/n and each X_i equals n(1+δ) with probability 1/(n(1+δ)) and 0 otherwise, then P(Z<T)=(1−1/(n(1+δ)))^n; this family tends to exp(−1/(1+δ)) as n→∞ and approaches 1/e as δ↓0, showing no constant larger than 1/e can be valid in Feige's conjecture. For Samuels' product, equality for any fixed i is obtained by setting X_j=1 for j>i and, for j≤i, taking X_j=0 with probability 1−μ_j/D and X_j=D/μ_j with probability μ_j/D, where D=T−∑_{k=i+1}^n μ_k; then the event Z<T occurs exactly when all first i variables are zero, giving probability ∏_{j=1}^i (1−μ_j/D). The paper cites Feige [1,2] for the original result with constant 1/13 in place of 1/e and Samuels [3] for the product-form conjecture.
why_relevant: Studies weighted sums of independent nonnegative variables and the relationship between Samuels' and Feige's conjectures. The notation and extremal reductions closely match the scaled form of the present problem.

title: An Elementary Analysis of the Probability That a Binomial Random Variable Exceeds Its Expectation
overall_summary: The paper proves elementary lower bounds on the probability that a binomial random variable is above its mean, most importantly an explicit estimate Pr[X > E[X]] > 1/2 − sqrt(n/(2π k(n−k))) for X∼Bin(n,p) with k=floor(np), and a uniform consequence Pr[X > E[X]] ≥ 1/4 whenever p ≥ ln(4/3)/n. The core method is to reduce arbitrary p to the grid value k/n by a simple stochastic-domination coupling, then use the fact that an integral binomial mean is a median and subtract an explicit Stirling-based upper bound on the point mass at the mean. Boundary and small-parameter cases are handled by elementary monotonicity estimates for expressions of the form (1±1/x)^{x+c}, plus finite checks propagated by monotonicity of the explicit bound. For the original weighted Bernoulli problem, the equal-weight case is exactly binomial, so these results provide rigorous lower estimates and possible extremal/counterexample tests; the local point-mass and asymptotic 1/2 estimates are also relevant when many weights are small and a binomial or normal approximation is plausible.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Rigollet--Tong lower bound and grid monotonicity, quoted in Introduction as equations (1) and (2)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X∼Bin(n,p). The paper reports that Rigollet and Tong stated Pr[X ≥ E[X]] ≥ min{p, 1/4} for p≤1/2, and that their proof actually gives the two-case bound Pr[X ≥ E[X]] ≥ 1/4 when p∈[1/n, 1/2], and Pr[X ≥ E[X]] ≥ p when p<1/n. A key auxiliary inequality in that proof is: for every integer k with 2≤k≤n/2, Pr[Bin(n,k/n) ≥ k+1] ≥ Pr[Bin(n,(k−1)/n) ≥ k].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Doerr does not reproduce the full proof. He states that the main work is the grid inequality above, proved through a connection between binomial distributions and order statistics of uniform distributions as in Feller. The argument is then reduced to proving the integral inequality k ∫_0^{(k−1)/n} t^{k−1}(1−t)^{n−k} dt ≤ (n−k) ∫_0^{k/n} t^k(1−t)^{n−k−1} dt. This grid inequality compares successive grid parameters (k−1)/n and k/n and can be iterated in the lower half of the distribution, together with the trivial small-p case E[X]<1. Doerr notes that this method does not obviously extend to p>1/2; in fact neither this inequality nor its reverse holds for all k in the upper half k∈[n/2..n−1].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1, Greenberg--Mohri bound, quoted
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n be a positive integer, let 1/n < p ≤ 1, and let X∼Bin(n,p). Then Pr[X ≥ E[X]] > 1/4.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this as a previous result and does not give the proof. It says that Greenberg and Mohri's proof is non-elementary and uses the Camp--Paulson normal approximation to the binomial cumulative distribution function. This result is relevant as a full-range non-strict-above-mean lower bound, in contrast to Doerr's elementary strict-above-mean estimates.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2, Pelekis--Ramon bound, quoted
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n be a positive integer, let 1/n ≤ p ≤ 1−1/n, let X∼Bin(n,p), and set a=np(1−p). Then Pr[X ≥ E[X]] ≥ (1/(2√2)) · sqrt(a)/(sqrt(a+1)+1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this as prior work and does not reproduce the proof. It states that the proof uses non-elementary stochastic-order tools, including the hazard rate order and likelihood ratio order for integer-valued distributions. Doerr observes that this bound improves the 1/4 Greenberg--Mohri bound only when np(1−p)>8 and that it never exceeds the limiting value 1/(2√2)≈0.3536.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3, elementary monotonicity estimates
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For real x in the indicated ranges and any α≥0: (a) the function (1−1/x)^{x−0.5+α} is increasing for x≥1; (b) the function (1+1/x)^{x+0.5+α} is decreasing for x>0; (c) the function (1−1/x)^x + (1−1/x)^{x−1} = 2(1−1/(2x))(1−1/x)^{x−1} is decreasing for x≥1. The convention 0^0=1 is used.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (a), it suffices to handle α=0, since the extra factor (1−1/x)^α is increasing for α≥0. At x=1 the function is 0, so for x>1 one studies the logarithm f_log(x)=(x−1/2) log(1−1/x). Expanding log(1−1/x)=−Σ_{i≥1} 1/(i x^i), the paper obtains f_log(x)=−1−Σ_{i≥1} ((i−1)/(2i(i+1))) x^{−i}, a constant plus increasing functions of x because the coefficients are nonnegative and the terms are negative powers with a minus sign. For (b), take reciprocals: 1/(1+1/x)^{x+0.5+α} = (1−1/(x+1))^{(x+1)−0.5+α}, which is increasing by (a), hence the original expression is decreasing. For (c), by continuity it is enough to take x>1 and study log((1/2)f(x)) = log(1−1/(2x)) + (x−1)log(1−1/x). Series expansion gives −1 plus a sum of nonnegative multiples of x^{−i}, hence this logarithm is decreasing; therefore f is decreasing.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4, Robbins form of Stirling's formula
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every positive integer n, n! = sqrt(2π n) (n/e)^n R_n, where the correction factor R_n satisfies 1 < exp(1/(12n+1)) < R_n < exp(1/(12n)) < 1.08690405.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is quoted from Robbins and is not proved in the paper. It is used as a precise elementary Stirling estimate with explicit one-sided error terms. The later binomial-coefficient and point-mass estimates follow by substituting this formula for n!, k!, and (n−k)! and collecting the correction factors.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5, explicit binomial-coefficient estimate
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For positive integers n and k with 1≤k≤n−1, C(n,k) = (1/sqrt(2π)) sqrt(n/(k(n−k))) (n/k)^k (n/(n−k))^{n−k} R_{n,k}, where the correction factor R_{n,k} satisfies exp(−1/6+1/25)=0.88102729... ≤ exp(−1/(12k) − 1/(12(n−k)) + 1/(12n+1)) < R_{n,k} < exp(−1/(12k+1) − 1/(12(n−k)+1) + 1/(12n)) < 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Robbins' formula to n!, k!, and (n−k)! in C(n,k)=n!/(k!(n−k)!). The main Stirling terms simplify to the displayed prefactor and powers (n/k)^k(n/(n−k))^{n−k}. The correction factor is R_n/(R_k R_{n−k}). The lower bound uses R_n>exp(1/(12n+1)) and R_k,R_{n−k}<exp(1/(12k)), exp(1/(12(n−k))); the upper bound uses R_n<exp(1/(12n)) and R_k,R_{n−k}>exp(1/(12k+1)), exp(1/(12(n−k)+1)). The crude numerical lower bound exp(−1/6+1/25) is uniform over 1≤k≤n−1; the upper exponent is always negative, giving R_{n,k}<1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6, uniform upper bound on a binomial point mass
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n be a positive integer, let k be an integer with 1≤k≤n−1, and let X∼Bin(n,p) for some p∈[0,1]. Then Pr[X=k] < (1/sqrt(2π)) sqrt(n/(k(n−k))). In particular, for Y∼Bin(n,k/n), the same strict bound holds for Pr[Y=k].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For fixed n and k, the binomial point probability C(n,k)p^k(1−p)^{n−k} is maximized at p=k/n, so Pr[X=k]≤Pr[Y=k] for Y∼Bin(n,k/n). Corollary 5 gives C(n,k)(k/n)^k(1−k/n)^{n−k} = (1/sqrt(2π))sqrt(n/(k(n−k))) R_{n,k}. Since Corollary 5 gives R_{n,k}<1, the strict upper bound follows.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7, stochastic domination of binomials with different success probabilities
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n be a positive integer and 0≤p<q≤1. If X∼Bin(n,p) and Y∼Bin(n,q), then for every integer k with 1≤k≤n, Pr[X≥k] < Pr[Y≥k]; for k=0 both probabilities equal 1. The paper states the range k∈[0..n], but the strict inequality is only meaningful and true for positive k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take independent random variables R_1,...,R_n uniformly distributed on [0,1). Define X_tilde=|{i: R_i<p}| and Y_tilde=|{i: R_i<q}|. Then X_tilde∼Bin(n,p), Y_tilde∼Bin(n,q), and X_tilde≤Y_tilde pointwise. Hence Pr[X≥k]≤Pr[Y≥k]. For k≥1 the inequality is strict because there is positive probability that enough of the R_i fall in the interval [p,q) to make Y_tilde≥k while X_tilde<k; boundary cases q=1 or p=0 can be handled directly, and the same coupling gives the required strict domination.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8, explicit lower bound for strict exceedance of the mean
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n be a positive integer, let 1/n≤p<1, let X∼Bin(n,p), and set k=floor(np). Then 1≤k≤n−1 and Pr[X > E[X]] = Pr[X≥k+1] > 1/2 − sqrt(n/(2π k(n−k))).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since k=floor(np), the strict event X>np is exactly X≥k+1. Let Y∼Bin(n,k/n). Because k/n≤p, Lemma 7 gives Pr[X≥k+1]≥Pr[Y≥k+1]. For Y, the expectation is the integer k. The paper invokes the known fact that a binomial distribution with integral expectation has that expectation as a median, so Pr[Y≥k]≥1/2. Therefore Pr[Y≥k+1]=Pr[Y≥k]−Pr[Y=k]≥1/2−Pr[Y=k]. Lemma 6 bounds Pr[Y=k] by sqrt(n/(2π k(n−k))), giving the displayed strict inequality. The paper further notes that the right side tends to 1/2 whenever k and n−k, equivalently np and n(1−p) along grid values, tend to infinity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Post-Lemma 8 observation and Corollary 9, constant lower bound when p is at least order 1/n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: After Lemma 8 the paper observes that, for fixed n, g(n,k)=1/2−sqrt(n/(2π k(n−k))) is minimized at k=1 and k=n−1, and g(n,1)=g(n,n−1) increases with n. Hence for n≥3, 1/n≤p<1, and k=floor(np), Lemma 8 gives Pr[X>E[X]]>g(3,1)>0.0113; for n=2 and p≥1/2, Pr[X>E[X]]≥Pr[X=2]=p^2≥1/4. The proof of Corollary 9 establishes the following corrected form: for every ε>0, every positive integer n, every p with ε/n≤p<1, and X∼Bin(n,p), Pr[X>E[X]]>min{1−exp(−ε), 0.0113}. The displayed corollary in the supplied text reads min{exp(−ε),0.0113}, but the proof uses 1−exp(−pn), so the proof-given bound is min{1−exp(−ε),0.0113}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For p≥1/n, use the monotonicity properties of g just described and Lemma 8, with the separate n=2 calculation. For 0<p<1/n, E[X]=np<1, so the event X>E[X] is X≥1. Thus Pr[X>E[X]]=1−Pr[X=0]=1−(1−p)^n. Using 1+x<e^x for x≠0 gives (1−p)^n<e^{−pn}; since p≥ε/n, this is at most e^{−ε}, yielding Pr[X>E[X]]>1−e^{−ε}. Combining the p≥1/n and p<1/n regimes gives the minimum of the two constants.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 10, uniform 1/4 bound for strict exceedance
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n be a positive integer, let α=ln(4/3)<0.2877, let α/n≤p<1, and let X∼Bin(n,p). Then Pr[X>E[X]]≥1/4. The paper also explains after the proof that the strict inequality Pr[X>E[X]]>1/4 holds for all such n,p except at (n,p)=(2,1/2), where Pr[X>E[X]]=1/4.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First handle α/n≤p<1/n. Then E[X]=np<1 and X>E[X] is X≥1, so Pr[X>E[X]]=1−(1−p)^n>1−e^{−pn}≥1−e^{−α}=1/4. Now assume p≥1/n and put k=floor(np). Let Y∼Bin(n,k/n). Lemma 7 gives Pr[X>E[X]]=Pr[X≥k+1]≥Pr[Y≥k+1], so it suffices to prove, for all positive n and all integers 1≤k≤n−1, that Pr[Bin(n,k/n)≥k+1]≥1/4. The proof then checks boundary k-values explicitly. For k=1 and n≥3, Pr[X≥2]=1−(1−1/n)^n−(1−1/n)^{n−1}≥7/27>0.2592, using Lemma 3(c). For k=2 and n≥5, Pr[X≥3]=1−(1−2/n)^n−2(1−2/n)^{n−1}−2(1−1/n)(1−2/n)^{n−2}=1−(1−2/n)^{n−1}(5+4/((n−2)n))>1−e^{−2}(5+4/15)>0.2872; here Lemma 3(a) shows the needed monotonicity of (1−2/n)^{n−1}. For k=n−1 and n≥2, Pr[X≥n]=(1−1/n)^n≥1/4. For k=n−2 and n≥4, Pr[X≥n−1]=2(1−2/n)^{n−1}+(1−2/n)^n≥2(1/2)^3+(1/2)^4=0.3125. For k=n−3 and n≥6, Pr[X≥n−2]=(9/2)(1−1/n)(1−3/n)^{n−2}+3(1−3/n)^{n−1}+(1−3/n)^n is bounded below by replacing 1−1/n with 1−3/n and then applying Lemma 3, giving at least 0.25. These cases cover k∈{1,2,n−3,n−2,n−1}. For the middle range, Lemma 8 gives the lower bound g(n,k). Since g(20,3)=g(20,17)>0.25 and k↦g(n,k) is concave/symmetric enough that this covers all k∈[3..17] at n=20, and since g(n+1,k)>g(n,k) and g(n+1,k+1)>g(n,k), all cases n≥20 and 3≤k≤n−3 follow. It remains to handle n≤19 and 3≤k≤n−4. The paper gives a human-readable finite check: for k=3 and n≥7 it bounds Pr[X≤3] by the first four binomial masses, uses Lemma 3 to show the first two are at most e^{−3} and 3e^{−3}, and shows the last two combined are decreasing in n and at most their n=7 value, giving Pr[X≥4]≥1−4e^{−3}−124416/235298≥0.2720. For k=n−4 and n≥8, writing the upper tail as the probability of at most three failures gives terms with coefficients 4^3/6, 8, 4, and 1; after a simple lower replacement and Lemma 3 monotonicity, the n=8 value gives at least 0.2903. The remaining middle cases are propagated from g(12,4)=g(12,8)>0.25 and g(11,5)=g(11,6)>0.25 via the same monotonicity in (n,k), leaving only (n,k)=(9,4),(10,4),(11,4),(10,5), which are checked directly; their actual probabilities Pr[Bin(n,k/n)≥k+1] are approximately 0.3655, 0.3668, 0.3678, and 0.3769, respectively. Strictness follows because the only non-strict boundary in these estimates is the grid case (n,k)=(2,1), and Lemma 7 gives strict improvement whenever p is not one of the grid values 1/n,2/n,...,(n−1)/n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 11, Pelekis lower bound for general upper binomial tails, quoted
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n be a positive integer, let 0<p<1, let X∼Bin(n,p), and let k be an integer with np<k≤n−1. Define ℓ=floor((k−np)/(1−p)). Then the quoted Pelekis bound is Pr[X≥k] ≥ (p^{2ℓ+2}/2) · C(n,ℓ+1)/C(k,ℓ+1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this theorem for comparison with its own bounds on exceeding the expectation by more than one and does not prove it. It says the result comes from Pelekis' approach via tail conditional expectations. In the paper it is used only as a benchmark for Figure 3, not as an ingredient in Doerr's proofs.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 12, exceeding the expectation by more than one
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n≥3 be an integer, let 1/n≤p<1−1/n, let X∼Bin(n,p), and set k=floor(np). Then 1≤k≤n−2 and: (a) Pr[X>E[X]+1] ≥ 0.5 − 2 sqrt(n/(2π k(n−k))); (b) Pr[X>E[X]+1] ≥ 0.25 − sqrt(n/(2π (k+1)(n−k−1))) · (1−1/(k+1))^{k+1} · (1+1/(n−k−1))^{n−k−1}; (c) Pr[X>E[X]+1] ≥ 0.0370.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: As in Lemma 8, reduce to the grid p=k/n. Indeed X>E[X]+1 is X≥k+2, and Lemma 7 gives Pr[Bin(n,p)≥k+2]≥Pr[Bin(n,k/n)≥k+2]. Assume henceforth X∼Bin(n,k/n). For (a), write Pr[X≥k+2]=Pr[X≥k]−Pr[X=k]−Pr[X=k+1]. The integer mean k is a median, so Pr[X≥k]≥1/2. Also k is a mode, or directly by computation Pr[X=k]≥Pr[X=k+1]. Lemma 6 then gives Pr[X≥k+2]>1/2−2 sqrt(n/(2π k(n−k))). For (b), use Theorem 10 at the grid value k/n to get Pr[X>E[X]]=Pr[X≥k+1]≥1/4, then subtract Pr[X=k+1]. Corollary 5 applied at r=k+1 bounds this point mass by sqrt(n/(2π (k+1)(n−k−1))) (k/(k+1))^{k+1} ((n−k)/(n−k−1))^{n−k−1}, which is exactly the displayed factor with 1−1/(k+1) and 1+1/(n−k−1). For (c), define the lower bound in (b) as h(n,k). The paper first shows h(n,k) is increasing in n for fixed k: the n-dependent factor can be rewritten as sqrt(n/(n−k)) (1+1/(n−k−1))^{n−k−1+0.5}, a product of decreasing positive factors by Lemma 3(b), so subtracting it makes h increase. Direct numerical checks give h(6,1), h(9,2), h(9,3), h(10,4), and h(10,5) all greater than 0.0370, covering all larger n with these fixed k. A second monotonicity argument handles the diagonal k=n−5 for n≥10: the relevant factor sqrt(n/(n−4))(1−1/(n−4))^{n−4} is decomposed as (1+4/(n−4))^{1/2}(1−1/(n−4))(1−1/(n−4))^{(n−4)−1}; Lemma 3 and a direct check of the squared first two factors show it decreases for n−4≥6, so h(n,n−5) increases from h(10,5)>0.0370. This covers all cases with k up to the diagonal region. The remaining near-top cases are evaluated directly: q(n,n−2)=(1−2/n)^n≥0.0370, q(n,n−3)=(1−3/n)^n+3(1−3/n)^{n−1}≥0.0507, and q(n,n−4)=(1−4/n)^n+4(1−4/n)^{n−1}+8(1−1/n)(1−4/n)^{n−2}≥0.0579; Lemma 3(a) supplies monotonicity and the first admissible n gives the stated constants. The only remaining cases (7,2), (8,2), (8,3), and (9,4) are checked directly, with probabilities approximately 0.1082, 0.1138, 0.1374, and 0.1573.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Post-Theorem 12 small-p observation for exceeding the mean by more than one
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For p=α/n<1/n, so α<1, and X∼Bin(n,p), the paper notes that Pr[X>E[X]+1] = 1−(1−p)^n−np(1−p)^{n−1} = 1−(1−α/n)^n−α(1−α/n)^{n−1} ≥ 1−exp(−α)−α exp(−α(n−1)/n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If p<1/n then E[X]=np=α<1, so X>E[X]+1 means X>α+1 with an integer-valued X, hence X≥2. Therefore the probability is 1−Pr[X=0]−Pr[X=1], giving the exact formula. The lower bound follows by using (1−α/n)^n≤exp(−α) and (1−α/n)^{n−1}≤exp(−α(n−1)/n).

proof_techniques.name: Uniform coupling for stochastic domination
proof_techniques.description: To compare Bin(n,p) and Bin(n,q) with p<q, the paper uses the same independent uniform random variables R_1,...,R_n and counts how many fall below p or q. This gives a pointwise inequality between the two counts and, for positive thresholds, a strict tail inequality. The proof is elementary and avoids differentiating binomial tail probabilities or invoking stochastic orders.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For equal weights, or for any fixed increasing event in Bernoulli coordinates, the same coupling compares probabilities as p changes. It is especially useful for reducing a binomial tail with arbitrary p to the grid point floor(np)/n where the mean is an integer.

proof_techniques.name: Floor-mean grid reduction
proof_techniques.description: For X∼Bin(n,p), putting k=floor(np) converts the strict-above-mean event X>np into X≥k+1. Since this threshold is fixed while p ranges over [k/n,(k+1)/n), stochastic domination shows the smallest such probability on the interval occurs at the left grid point p=k/n. The paper repeatedly reduces arbitrary p to X∼Bin(n,k/n), where E[X]=k is integral.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In the equal-weight version of the original problem, the event (1/n)X≥p is a binomial threshold event whose threshold is constant on intervals between grid points. An advisor can try to analyze each interval by reducing to the endpoint grid distribution, adjusting for the difference between ≥ and > when np is integral.

proof_techniques.name: Median minus local mass
proof_techniques.description: At the grid distribution Bin(n,k/n), the mean k is a median, so Pr[X≥k]≥1/2. The strict upper tail is Pr[X≥k+1], which equals Pr[X≥k]−Pr[X=k]. Thus an upper bound on the central point mass immediately gives a lower bound on strict exceedance. For exceeding the mean by more than one, the same idea subtracts the masses at k and k+1, using that k is a mode.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the main bridge from median information to quantitative tail probability. For weighted Bernoulli sums, analogous strategies would need a median-at-mean substitute and an anti-concentration estimate near the threshold.

proof_techniques.name: Robbins-Stirling anti-concentration bound
proof_techniques.description: The paper derives from Robbins' Stirling formula the explicit estimate Pr[Bin(n,p)=k]≤Pr[Bin(n,k/n)=k] < sqrt(n/(2π k(n−k))). This is a local central-mass estimate with fully explicit constants and no asymptotic notation. It drives the bound tending to 1/2 when both k and n−k grow.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For equal weights, this directly quantifies how much probability can sit exactly at the threshold. In many-small-weight regimes, similar local limit or anti-concentration estimates may control the loss when passing from ≥ mean to > mean.

proof_techniques.name: Elementary log-series monotonicity checks
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3 proves monotonicity of expressions such as (1−1/x)^{x−1/2+α}, (1+1/x)^{x+1/2+α}, and (1−1/x)^x+(1−1/x)^{x−1} by expanding logarithms into power series. These monotonicities allow the paper to replace n-dependent boundary-case probabilities by their first admissible value or their limiting value.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proposed solution reduces to checking finitely many binomial or near-binomial boundary cases, these monotonicity tools can turn many cases into a single endpoint computation without numerical black boxes.

proof_techniques.name: Finite seed checks propagated by an explicit lattice bound
proof_techniques.description: The function g(n,k)=1/2−sqrt(n/(2π k(n−k))) has useful monotonicity: g(n+1,k)>g(n,k) and g(n+1,k+1)>g(n,k), and for fixed n its worst cases are near the edges. The paper checks a small set of seed values such as g(20,3)>1/4 and propagates them to all larger n,k in a lattice region; only a small residual finite set remains for direct computation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests a proof architecture for determining p-ranges: combine an analytic bound for the bulk with exact or rational checks for finitely many small/eccentric equal-weight cases, then use monotonicity to avoid exhaustive enumeration.

proof_techniques.name: Strictness from non-grid parameters
proof_techniques.description: When p is not exactly a grid value j/n, the reduction from p to floor(np)/n is strict by stochastic domination. The paper uses this to sharpen Theorem 10 from ≥1/4 to >1/4 except for the single grid case (n,p)=(2,1/2).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem uses a non-strict threshold ≥p, so equality cases may matter. This grid/non-grid distinction is a useful way to isolate potential extremal parameter values in equal-weight tests.

other_useful_info: The paper emphasizes that Doerr's bound Pr[X>E[X]] > 1/2−sqrt(n/(2π k(n−k))) approaches 1/2 whenever np and n(1−p) tend to infinity. For equal weights, the distinction between Pr[X>np] and Pr[X≥np] is important: the paper's strongest elementary uniform theorem is for strict exceedance, while Greenberg--Mohri's quoted theorem is for non-strict exceedance. Theorem 10 gives a strict 1/4 lower bound for p≥ln(4/3)/n except at (n,p)=(2,1/2), where the strict probability is exactly 1/4. For p<1/n, the simple identities Pr[X>E[X]]=1−(1−p)^n and Pr[X>E[X]+1]=1−(1−p)^n−np(1−p)^{n−1} are explicitly used. The introduction notes that the Rigollet--Tong monotonicity inequality works naturally only for p≤1/2 and that neither it nor its reverse holds uniformly in the upper half, which may be relevant when searching for equal-weight extremal examples at larger p.
why_relevant: Provides elementary, explicit estimates for binomial probabilities above the expectation. These estimates are useful both for equal-weight counterexamples and for approximating many-small-weight regimes.

title: Small Deviations of Sums of Independent Random Variables
overall_summary: The paper improves Feige-type small-deviation lower bounds for sums of independent nonnegative random variables with individual means at most 1, proving Pr[Σ_i X_i < E[Σ_i X_i] + 1] ≥ 7/50, and in fact a slightly better explicit constant. The core method is a fourth-moment duality argument: construct explicit quartic polynomials Q_{ℓ,r} majorizing one-sided indicators, then optimize their expectations using first through fourth moment estimates and, crucially, the sign of the third central moment. The paper proves several reusable one-sided bounds for 4-wise independent bounded mean-zero sums, including refinements when the third moment is nonnegative and when the summands are two-point with nonpositive third moment. It also gives exact linear-programming dual calculations for tail probabilities of 2-wise and 3-wise independent Bernoulli sums, yielding counterexamples to small-deviation bounds under only pairwise or 3-wise independence. For the original weighted Bernoulli problem, the most relevant ingredients are the quartic indicator majorants, the two-point moment identities, the split according to third-moment sign, and the Bernoulli linear-programming viewpoint.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2 and equation (11): quartic one-sided majorant Q_{ℓ,r}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ℓ>0 and r>0. Define Q_{ℓ,r}(x)=Σ_{i=0}^4 q_i x^i, where q_0=1, q_1=2r^2(2ℓ+r)/(ℓ(ℓ+r)^3), q_2=r(-8ℓ^2-ℓr+r^2)/(ℓ^2(ℓ+r)^3), q_3=(4ℓ^2-4ℓr-2r^2)/(ℓ^2(ℓ+r)^3), and q_4=(3ℓ+r)/(ℓ^2(ℓ+r)^3). Then Q_{ℓ,r}(x) ≥ 1{x≥0} for every real x. If X is a real random variable with E[X]=0 and finite fourth moment, then for every δ>0, E[Q_{ℓ,r}(X-δ)] = Σ_{i=0}^4 (-δ)^i q_i + (q_2-3δq_3+6δ^2q_4)E[X^2] + (q_3-4δq_4)E[X^3] + q_4E[X^4]. Also q_4>0 always, and q_3<0 whenever ℓ≤(1+sqrt(3))r/2; in particular q_3<0 when ℓ≤r. In the special case ℓ=r, denoting the polynomial by Q_r, one has Q_r(x)=1+3x/(4r)-x^2/r^2-x^3/(4r^3)+x^4/(2r^4).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by explicit factorization. The polynomial can be written as Q_{ℓ,r}(x) = ((ℓ+x)^2/((ℓ^2)(ℓ+r)^3))*((ℓ+r)^3 - 2(ℓ^2+3ℓr+r^2)x + (3ℓ+r)x^2). The quadratic factor has minimum at x=(ℓ^2+3ℓr+r^2)/(3ℓ+r), and the corresponding numerator is ((3ℓ+r)(ℓ+r)^3-(ℓ^2+3ℓr+r^2)^2)/(3ℓ+r) = (2ℓ^4+4ℓ^3r+ℓ^2r^2)/(3ℓ+r), which is nonnegative. Hence Q_{ℓ,r}(x)≥0 for all x. A second factorization gives Q_{ℓ,r}(x)-1 = (x(4ℓ^2+2ℓr+(3ℓ+r)x)(x-r)^2)/(ℓ^2(ℓ+r)^3), which is nonnegative for x≥0; combining this with Q_{ℓ,r}≥0 proves Q_{ℓ,r}≥1{x≥0}. The expectation identity follows by expanding Q_{ℓ,r}(X-δ), using E[X]=0, and collecting coefficients of E[X^2], E[X^3], and E[X^4]. The sign facts follow immediately from the displayed formulas for q_3 and q_4.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2 / Theorem 2.1: moment bound with nonnegative third moment
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a real random variable with E[X]=0, E[X^2]=σ^2, and E[X^3]≥0. If E[X^4]≤cσ^4, then Pr[X≥0]≤1-1/(2c).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the quartic majorant Q_r with r=sqrt(c)σ, namely Q(x)=1+3x/(4sqrt(c)σ)-x^2/(cσ^2)-x^3/(4c^{3/2}σ^3)+x^4/(2c^2σ^4). By Lemma 2.2, 1{x≥0}≤Q(x), so Pr[X≥0]≤E[Q(X)]. Since E[X]=0, the linear term vanishes. The quadratic contribution is -E[X^2]/(cσ^2)=-1/c. The cubic coefficient is negative and E[X^3]≥0, so the cubic term is nonpositive and can be dropped for an upper bound. The fourth term is at most E[X^4]/(2c^2σ^4)≤1/(2c). Thus E[Q(X)]≤1-1/c+1/(2c)=1-1/(2c). The paper notes tightness: for any a>0 and p<1/2, the law with mass p at -a, mass 1-2p at 0, and mass p at a has E[X^3]=0 and attains equality for the corresponding value of c.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.1 moment estimates for bounded centered sums
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X_1,...,X_n be independent mean-zero random variables, let X=Σ_i X_i, and set σ^2=E[X^2]. If |X_i|≤1 for every i, then |E[X^3]|≤σ^2 and E[X^4]≤3σ^4+σ^2. The paper observes that the same moment computations only require 4-wise independence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because the summands are centered and independent, all mixed third-moment terms vanish, so E[X^3]=Σ_i E[X_i^3]. Since |X_i|≤1, |E[X_i^3]|≤E[|X_i|^3]≤E[X_i^2], and summing gives |E[X^3]|≤Σ_i E[X_i^2]=E[X^2]=σ^2. For the fourth moment, independence and centering give E[X^4]=Σ_i E[X_i^4]+6Σ_{i<j}E[X_i^2]E[X_j^2]. Rewriting, E[X^4]=3(Σ_i E[X_i^2])^2+Σ_i(E[X_i^4]-3E[X_i^2]^2). Since |X_i|≤1 implies E[X_i^4]≤E[X_i^2], the last display is at most 3σ^4+Σ_i E[X_i^2]=3σ^4+σ^2. Only joint moments involving at most four variables are used, hence 4-wise independence suffices.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.3 / Theorem 2.3: bounded 4-wise independent small deviation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X_1,...,X_n be a 4-wise independent collection of random variables such that E[X_i]=0 and |X_i|≤1 for every i. Let X=Σ_i X_i. Then Pr[X<δ]≥1/6 for every δ≥1/3; equivalently, Pr[X≥1/3]≤5/6.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let σ^2=E[X^2]. The bounded-sum moment estimates give E[X^3]≥-σ^2 and E[X^4]≤3σ^4+σ^2. Apply the majorant Q_{ℓ,r} to X-δ with δ=1/3. For choices with ℓ≤r, q_3<0 and q_4>0, so q_3-4δq_4<0; replacing E[X^3] by its lower bound -σ^2 and E[X^4] by its upper bound 3σ^4+σ^2 yields an explicit upper bound for E[Q_{ℓ,r}(X-1/3)]. If ℓ=r=sqrt(3)σ, equation (15) gives E[Q_{sqrt(3)σ}(X-1/3)]≤5/6 + (1/(36σ^4))*(2/81 + (sqrt(3)/27)σ + (14/3)σ^2 - sqrt(3)σ^3), which is ≤5/6 for σ≥3. For intermediate σ, the paper sets r=aσ and ℓ=r, obtaining B_a(σ)=(3-2a^2+2a^4)/(2a^4)+(2-a^2)/(4a^3)σ^{-1}+(27-2a^2)/(18a^4)σ^{-2}+1/(108a^3)σ^{-3}+1/(162a^4)σ^{-4}. When 27-2a^2≥0 this is convex as a polynomial in σ^{-1}; checking endpoints proves B_2≤5/6 on σ∈[3/2,3], B_{9/4}≤5/6 on σ∈[1,3/2], and B_{5/2}≤5/6 on σ∈[1/2,1]. Finally, for σ<1/2 the fixed choice r=3/2 gives E[Q_{3/2}(X-1/3)]≤10339/13122+(8/27)σ^4≤5/6. Since Q majorizes 1{x≥0}, E[Q(X-1/3)] bounds Pr[X≥1/3], completing the proof.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1: positive-third-moment refinement
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X_1,...,X_n be a 4-wise independent collection of random variables such that E[X_i]=0 and |X_i|≤1 for every i. Let X=Σ_i X_i. If E[X^3]≥0, then Pr[X≥4/25]≤5/6, equivalently Pr[X<4/25]≥1/6.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Again set σ^2=E[X^2]. The fourth moment estimate gives E[X^4]≤3σ^4+σ^2. In the expectation identity for Q_{ℓ,r}(X-δ), take δ=4/25 and choose ℓ≤r, so q_3-4δq_4<0. Since E[X^3]≥0, the cubic contribution is nonpositive and can be discarded rather than bounded by -σ^2; this is the key improvement over Theorem 2.3. With ℓ=r=sqrt(3)σ, the paper obtains E[Q_{sqrt(3)σ}(X-δ)]≤5/6 + (2δ^4+sqrt(3)δ^3σ+2σ^2-6sqrt(3)δσ^3)/(36σ^4), and for δ=4/25 this is ≤5/6 for σ>5/4.
