For σ∈[0.68,1.25], it takes ℓ=2σ and r=5σ/2 and obtains the upper bound 835/972 - (226δ/729)σ^{-1} + ((68-207δ^2)/2916)σ^{-2} + (11δ^3/243)σ^{-3} + (17δ^4/729)σ^{-4}, which is convex in σ^{-1} for δ=4/25 and is checked at the two endpoints. For σ∈[0.5,0.68], it takes ℓ=15σ/7 and r=3σ and obtains 256957/291600 - (10633δ/32400)σ^{-1} + ((26411-128625δ^2)/1749600)σ^{-2} + (7889δ^3/194400)σ^{-3} + (26411δ^4/1749600)σ^{-4}, again convex in σ^{-1} and endpoint-checked. For σ∈[0,0.5], it uses ℓ=1 and r=2, giving E[Q_{1,2}(X-4/25)]≤62573/78125 - (59/3375)σ^2 + (5/9)σ^4≤5/6. These cases cover all σ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2: two-point negative-third-moment refinement
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X_1,...,X_n be a 4-wise independent collection of mean-zero random variables. For each i, suppose X_i has support {-a_i,b_i}, where a_i>0 and b_i>0, and suppose a_i≤1 for every i. Assume b_1=max_i b_i and a_1≥1/16. Let X=Σ_i X_i. If E[X^3]≤0, then Pr[X≥1]≤5/6, equivalently Pr[X<1]≥1/6.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a mean-zero two-point variable with support {-a_i,b_i}, the probabilities are Pr[X_i=-a_i]=b_i/(a_i+b_i) and Pr[X_i=b_i]=a_i/(a_i+b_i). Hence E[X_i^2]=a_i b_i and E[X_i^3]=a_i b_i(b_i-a_i). Set σ^2=E[X^2]=Σ_i a_i b_i. Since a_i≤1, E[X^3]=Σ_i a_i b_i(b_i-a_i)≥-Σ_i a_i^2b_i≥-σ^2. For the fourth moment, the standard fourth-moment expansion gives E[X^4]≤3σ^4+Σ_i E[X_i^4], and the two-point formula is bounded by E[X_i^4]≤a_i b_i(a_i^2+b_i^2). Thus E[X^4]≤3σ^4+Σ_i a_i^3b_i+Σ_i a_i b_i^3≤3σ^4+σ^2+Σ_i a_i b_i^3. The assumption E[X^3]≤0 implies Σ_i a_i b_i^2≤Σ_i a_i^2b_i≤σ^2. Since b_1 is maximal and a_1≥1/16, Σ_i a_i b_i^3≤b_1Σ_i a_i b_i^2≤b_1σ^2≤4sqrt(Σ_i a_i b_i^2)σ^2≤4σ^3. Therefore E[X^4]≤3σ^4+4σ^3+σ^2. Applying the expectation identity to Q_{ℓ,r}(X-1) with ℓ=r choices and using E[X^3]≥-σ^2 gives E[Q_{ℓ,r}(X-1)]≤Σ_i(-1)^i q_i+(q_2-3q_3+6q_4)σ^2+(q_3-4q_4)(-σ^2)+q_4(3σ^4+4σ^3+σ^2). With r=ℓ=sqrt(3)σ this becomes at most 5/6 + (2+sqrt(3)σ+10σ^2+(8-5sqrt(3))σ^3)/(36σ^4), which is ≤5/6 for σ≥16. For σ∈[5/2,16], the paper chooses r=ℓ=19σ/10 and obtains (218442-24885σ^{-1}+37800σ^{-2}+9500σ^{-3}+10000σ^{-4})/260642, a convex polynomial in σ^{-1} that is endpoint-checked to be ≤5/6. For σ∈[0,5/2], it chooses r=ℓ=5 and obtains (1016-29σ^2+4σ^3+3σ^4)/1250≤5/6 by elementary calculus. Thus Pr[X≥1]≤E[Q(X-1)]≤5/6.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1 / Theorem 3.3: improved Feige-type small-deviation bound
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X_1,...,X_n be nonnegative independent random variables with means μ_i=E[X_i] satisfying μ_i≤1 for every i. Then Pr[Σ_i X_i < Σ_i μ_i + 1] ≥ β, where β=(46/279)e^{-4/25}; in particular β>7/50.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof begins with Feige's reductions, cited from [2]. By a linear-programming argument one may assume each X_i is nonconstant and has support of size two; subtracting the lower support point makes each support {0,c_i} without changing the target probability, possibly reducing μ_i. A merge operation combines the two variables of smallest means when one has mean below a threshold t and the other has mean at most 1-t, then again reduces to a two-point support at 0; in the proof t is used as 1/16, so at termination there is at most one mean below 1/16 and all other means exceed 15/16. Write s_i=c_i-μ_i and sort so s_1≥...≥s_n. Fix τ=25/4 and define k=max(0,max{j: s_j≥τ(μ_1+...+μ_j)}); let m=Σ_{i≤k}μ_i. For i>k, the stopping rule gives s_i≤τ(m+μ_{k+1})≤τ(m+1). For i≤k, s_i≥τm, so if k>0 then Pr[X_1=...=X_k=0]=Π_{i≤k}(1-μ_i/(s_i+μ_i))≥Π_{i≤k}exp(-μ_i/(τm))=e^{-1/τ}=e^{-4/25}. Conditioning on these first k variables being 0 reduces the problem to centered variables Y_j=X_{k+j}-μ_{k+j}, j=1,...,n-k, with supports {-a_j,b_j}, where a_j≤1 and b_j≤(25/4)(m+1). If E[(Σ_j Y_j)^3]≥0, scale Y_j by 4/(25(m+1)) and apply Lemma 3.1 to get Pr[Σ_j Y_j<m+1]≥1/6, hence the original probability is at least e^{-4/25}/6>β. If E[(Σ_j Y_j)^3]<0 and the largest-positive-support variable satisfies a_1≥1/16, Lemma 3.2 gives Pr[Σ_j Y_j<1]≥1/6 and hence Pr[Σ_j Y_j<m+1]≥1/6. If instead a_1<1/16, the merge stopping condition implies all other means are >15/16. If b_1<3(m+1), scaling by 1/(3(m+1)) and applying Theorem 2.3 again gives probability at least 1/6. Thus one may assume b_1≥3(m+1). Then Pr[Y_1=b_1]=a_1/(a_1+b_1)≤1/(48(m+1)); condition on Y_1 not taking this positive value, which only helps the upper-tail event. Removing Y_1 leaves the third moment still negative because E[Y_1^3]=a_1b_1(b_1-a_1)>0, and the remaining variables have a_j>1/16, so Lemma 3.2 applies. If k=0, this gives at least (1-1/48)/6>β. If k≥1, then m≥15/16, so the total lower bound is at least e^{-4/25}(1-1/(48(m+1)))(1/6)≥e^{-4/25}(92/93)(1/6)=β.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4, k=2 Bernoulli linear program and Theorem 1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A(n,2,p) be the set of collections of n pairwise independent Bernoulli random variables with common marginal probability p, let S be their sum, and let m be an integer threshold. In the regime m≤np+1-p, the maximum of Pr[S≥m] over A(n,2,p) equals p(n+m-np-1+p)/m. An optimal exchangeable distribution for S is supported on {0,m,n} with probabilities p_0=(1-p)(m-np+p)/m, p_m=p(1-p)n(n-1)/(m(n-m)), and p_n=p(np-m+1-p)/(n-m). Equivalently, when m=(n+d)p is an integer and p≤1/(d+1), this maximum is p(n+m-np-1+p)/m; it is maximized at p=1/(d+1), where p_0=d/(n+d), p_m=n/(n+d), and p_n=0. Consequently, Theorem 1.4 states: for δ>0 such that (n+δ)/(δ+1) is an integer, there exist nonnegative pairwise independent random variables X_1,...,X_n, each with mean 1, such that Pr[Σ_i X_i<n+δ]=δ/(n+δ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper formulates a linear program for k-wise independent Bernoulli variables. Because the event {S≥m} is symmetric, one may symmetrize and work with probabilities p_r=Pr[S=r], r=0,...,n. Pairwise independence with common marginal p is encoded by matching the first two moments of S to those of Bin(n,p): Σ_r r^i p_r=E[Bin(n,p)^i] for i=0,1,2. The dual minimizes E[Q(B)] for B~Bin(n,p) over degree-2 polynomials Q with Q(j)≥0 for j<m and Q(j)≥1 for j≥m. The polynomial f(x)=((m+n)x-x^2)/(mn) is feasible: f(0)=0, f(m)=f(n)=1, it is nonnegative below m, and by concavity it is at least 1 on the interval from m to n. Its binomial expectation equals p(n+m-np-1+p)/m. Complementary slackness with support at the points where f is 0 or 1 gives the displayed primal probabilities. For Theorem 1.4, set d=δ and p=1/(δ+1), choose pairwise independent Bernoulli variables B_i with the optimal law, and scale X_i=B_i/p=(δ+1)B_i. Then E[X_i]=1 and Σ_i X_i<n+δ is equivalent to Σ_i B_i<(n+δ)p=m, which has probability p_0=δ/(n+δ).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4, k=3 Bernoulli linear program and Theorem 1.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A(n,3,p) be the set of collections of n 3-wise independent Bernoulli random variables with common marginal probability p, let S be their sum, and let m be an integer threshold. In the regime m≤np+1-2p, the maximum of Pr[S≥m] over A(n,3,p) equals p((n-2)(1-p)^2+m(2-p))/m. The corresponding optimal distribution is supported on {0,m,n-1,n}. When m=(n+d)p is an integer and p≤1/(d+2), the maximum is maximized at p=1/(d+2), and the optimal probabilities simplify to p_0=(d+1)^2/((d+2)(n+d)), p_m=(d+1)n(n-1)/((n+d)(n+nd-d)), p_{n-1}=0, and p_n=1/((d+2)(n+nd-d)). Consequently, Theorem 1.5 states: for δ>0 such that (n+δ)/(δ+2) is an integer, there exist nonnegative 3-wise independent random variables X_1,...,X_n, each with mean 1, such that Pr[Σ_i X_i<n+δ]=((δ+1)^2)/((δ+2)(n+δ)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: As in the pairwise case, symmetrization reduces the primal problem to variables p_r=Pr[S=r] with moment constraints matching Bin(n,p) up to degree 3. The dual minimizes E[Q(B)] over cubic polynomials Q satisfying Q(j)≥0 below the threshold m and Q(j)≥1 at and above m. The paper gives the feasible cubic g(x)=((n^2+2mn-n-m)x-(2n+m-1)x^2+x^3)/(n(n-1)m), which satisfies g(0)=0 and g(m)=g(n-1)=g(n)=1; checking feasibility and evaluating E[g(B)] yields p((n-2)(1-p)^2+m(2-p))/m. Complementary slackness then identifies the optimal support {0,m,n-1,n} and the displayed probabilities in the special case p=1/(d+2). For Theorem 1.5, take d=δ, p=1/(δ+2), construct Bernoulli variables B_i with this optimal 3-wise independent law, and scale X_i=B_i/p=(δ+2)B_i. Then E[X_i]=1 and the event Σ_i X_i<n+δ is the same as Σ_i B_i<m, whose probability is p_0=((δ+1)^2)/((δ+2)(n+δ)).

proof_techniques.name: Moment-problem duality with polynomial majorants
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the dual of a truncated moment problem: to upper-bound Pr[X in S] from finitely many moments, find a low-degree polynomial Q that majorizes 1_S pointwise and then bound E[Q(X)] using the available moments. For one-sided events S={x≥δ}, it shifts the variable and uses the quartic Q_{ℓ,r}, designed to have a double root at -ℓ, value 1 at 0, and Q-1 with a double root at r. The factorization of Q and Q-1 gives pointwise feasibility, while the free parameters ℓ and r allow optimization in different variance regimes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a weighted Bernoulli sum, centering gives a sum of independent two-point mean-zero variables; polynomial majorants can bound either Pr[Σ w_i(v_i-p)≥0] or the complementary side after changing signs. The parameters ℓ,r could be optimized using the explicit Bernoulli moments as functions of p and the weights.

proof_techniques.name: Exploit the sign of the third central moment
proof_techniques.description: Unlike earlier fourth-moment-only arguments, the paper keeps the cubic term in E[Q(X)]. For the quartics used here, q_3 is negative when ℓ≤r, and after shifting by δ the coefficient q_3-4δq_4 remains negative. If E[X^3]≥0, this term improves the upper bound and can be discarded as nonpositive; if E[X^3]<0, it is controlled by lower bounds such as E[X^3]≥-σ^2 or by two-point structure.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For centered Bernoulli-type summands, the third central moment has a simple sign depending on the Bernoulli bias and the chosen orientation. Splitting the original problem into p≤1/2 and p≥1/2, or applying the argument to the negative centered sum, is a natural way to use this idea.

proof_techniques.name: Two-point moment identities and skewness control
proof_techniques.description: For a mean-zero variable supported on {-a,b}, the paper uses Pr[-a]=b/(a+b), Pr[b]=a/(a+b), E[X^2]=ab, and E[X^3]=ab(b-a). Under a nonpositive third moment assumption for a sum of such variables, it derives Σ_i a_i b_i^2≤Σ_i a_i^2 b_i≤σ^2. Combined with a lower bound on the a_i corresponding to the largest b_i, this controls the otherwise unbounded fourth-moment contribution Σ_i a_i b_i^3 by 4σ^3.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Each weighted Bernoulli summand after centering is exactly two-point. These identities allow one to express all low moments in terms of p and the weights and may help isolate or control heavy weights that dominate the tail probability.

proof_techniques.name: Variance-regime polynomial tuning
proof_techniques.description: The proofs do not use a single quartic for all variance values. They choose ℓ and r as multiples of σ for large and medium σ, and fixed ℓ,r for small σ. After substitution, the expectation bound is often a polynomial in σ^{-1}; when the coefficient of σ^{-2} is nonnegative, convexity in σ^{-1} reduces verification on an interval to checking endpoints.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If optimizing over weights reduces to controlling the variance σ^2=p(1-p)Σ_i w_i^2 or similar scale parameters, a case split between diffuse weights and heavy weights could make polynomial inequalities tractable.

proof_techniques.name: Conditioning away large-surplus variables
proof_techniques.description: In the main theorem, after reducing variables to two-point supports {0,c_i}, the proof sorts by surplus s_i=c_i-μ_i and chooses an initial block whose surpluses are large relative to their total mean. The event that all variables in this block are zero has probability at least e^{-1/τ}; conditioning on it leaves a remainder whose positive supports are bounded by τ times the allowed deviation. This allows bounded-sum lemmas to be applied to the remainder.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For weighted Bernoulli sums, large weights may be treated separately by conditioning on whether the corresponding Bernoulli variables are zero or one. This can reduce the problem to a bounded or more homogeneous remainder while paying an explicit probability factor.

proof_techniques.name: Merge/reduction to two-point variables
proof_techniques.description: The main theorem invokes Feige's linear-programming reduction showing that, for the small-deviation lower bound considered there, one may assume variables are nonconstant with two-point support, then shift the lower support to zero. A merge operation combines two small-mean variables without increasing the bad probability, producing a terminal configuration in which at most one mean is below a chosen threshold and all others are large.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem already has two-point summands, so the support reduction is not needed, but the merge philosophy suggests searching for extremal weight configurations with few distinct scales or with one exceptional small component.

proof_techniques.name: Linear programming duality for k-wise independent Bernoulli sums
proof_techniques.description: Section 4 formulates the maximum tail probability of a k-wise independent Bernoulli sum as a finite linear program in the probabilities p_r=Pr[S=r]. Moment constraints up to degree k match those of Bin(n,p), and the dual searches for degree-k polynomials that are nonnegative below the threshold and at least 1 above it. For k=2 and k=3, explicit optimal polynomials identify the optimal primal support by complementary slackness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original variables are fully independent and weighted, the same polynomial-dual mindset may help identify extremal distributions or certify inequalities for symmetric or equal-weight subcases. The k-wise examples also warn that low-moment information alone may be insufficient for sharp Bernoulli tail statements.

other_useful_info: The introduction recalls Feige's conjectured optimal constant 1/e for the bound Pr[Σ_i X_i < EΣ_i X_i + 1] under independent nonnegative variables with means at most 1; the paper gives the standard tightness example where each X_i has mean 1 and support {0,n+δ}. For smaller deviations, it records the example with one variable supported on {0,1+δ} and the rest constant, giving probability δ/(1+δ). The paper states that the 1/6 constant in Theorem 1.3 is best possible for the 4-wise independent bounded setting, and that the deviation threshold there cannot be lowered below 1/5; these tightness facts are attributed to Garnett's thesis [3]. It also remarks that, because Theorem 1.3 and the Section 3 lemmas are tight in that fourth-moment framework, one cannot obtain a constant larger than 1/6 in the main theorem using only first four moments; possible improvements would require either lowering the δ=4/25 in Lemma 3.1 or using higher even moments. The Bernoulli linear-programming setup in Section 4 is connected to Peled, Yadin, and Yehudayoff [8], and the two-point reduction/merge transformations are attributed to Feige [2].
why_relevant: Improves Feige-type small-deviation bounds using moment methods and discusses Samuels' conjecture and two-point/Bernoulli subclasses. The Bernoulli-specialized arguments are likely useful for optimizing over the weights.

title: On the Chvátal-Janson Conjecture
overall_summary: The paper proves, for every integer n≥2, the Chvátal-Janson statement about the sequence q_m=P(B(n,m/n)≤m), where B(n,m/n) is binomial with n trials and success probability m/n: q_m is minimized at the nearest integer to 2n/3, and the adjacent monotonicity is exactly q_m≥q_{m+1} iff m+1/2<2n/3. The proof represents q_m through uniform order statistics and beta integrals, reduces adjacent comparisons q_m≥q_{m+1} to a normalized one-dimensional integral inequality h_n(m)≥1, proves h_n(m) is decreasing in m, and then checks only the boundary cases according to n mod 3. The boundary estimates are elementary but sharp: lower bounds use Bernoulli's inequality and geometric-series bounds, while upper bounds use logarithmic series expansions with cancellation of linear terms, truncation after cubic terms, exp(c)<1+c+c^2/2 for c<0, and polynomial integration. For the original weighted Bernoulli problem, the paper is most directly useful for equal-weight configurations, where probabilities reduce to binomial tails at integer means; it supplies exact finite-n control of how those tails vary with the grid parameter m/n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Main theorem proved by the paper, all-n Chvátal-Janson monotonicity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n≥2 be an integer. For each m∈{0,1,...,n}, let B(n,m/n) denote a binomial random variable with n trials and success probability m/n, and define q_m=P(B(n,m/n)≤m). Then, for every m∈{0,1,...,n−1}, q_m≥q_{m+1} if and only if m+1/2<2n/3. Consequently the sequence q_0,...,q_n attains its minimum at m=[[2n/3]], where [[x]] denotes the nearest integer to x.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof starts from the order-statistic representation q_m=P(U_(m+1)>m/n), where U_(j) is the j-th order statistic of n independent Uniform[0,1] variables. This gives a beta-integral formula for q_m. Lemma 1 compares the beta-integral formulas for q_m and q_{m+1}; after integration by parts and changes of variables, for 1≤m≤n−2 the comparison q_m≥q_{m+1} becomes h_n(m)≥1, where h_n(m)=∫_0^1 (1−v/(m+1))^m (1+v/(n−m−1))^(n−m−1) dv. Lemma 2 proves that h_n(m) is decreasing in m, by differentiating the logarithm of the integrand with respect to real m and using elementary logarithmic inequalities. Thus, for fixed n, it is enough to check h_n(m) on the two indices around the predicted cutoff. Write n=3s+r with r∈{0,1,2}. If r=0, the cutoff is between m=2s−1 and m=2s, so it suffices to prove h_{3s}(2s−1)≥1 and h_{3s}(2s)<1. If r=1 or r=2, the cutoff is between m=2s and m=2s+1, so it suffices to prove h_{3s+r}(2s)≥1 and h_{3s+r}(2s+1)<1. These are exactly the boundary integral estimates displayed as equations (8), (9), and (10) in the paper. The estimates are proved by elementary inequalities: Bernoulli's inequality gives the lower-bound sides, while logarithmic series truncation and polynomial upper bounds give the upper-bound sides. Endpoint comparisons not covered by the h_n formula are direct: q_0=1, q_n=1, and q_{n−1}<q_n for n≥2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equations (1)-(2), order-statistic and beta-integral representation of q_m
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n≥2, let U_1,...,U_n be independent Uniform[0,1] random variables, and let U_(1)≤...≤U_(n) be their order statistics. For m∈{0,...,n−1}, define q_m=P(B(n,m/n)≤m). Then q_m=P(Σ_{i=1}^n 1_{U_i≤m/n}≤m)=P(U_(m+1)>m/n), and hence q_m=(m+1) binom(n,m+1) ∫_{m/n}^1 x^m(1−x)^(n−m−1) dx.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Couple B(n,m/n) as the number of uniforms U_i that fall in [0,m/n]. The event that at most m uniforms are at most m/n is equivalent to the event that the (m+1)-st order statistic exceeds m/n. The density of U_(m+1) is f_{m+1}(x)=(m+1) binom(n,m+1) x^m(1−x)^(n−m−1) on [0,1]. Integrating this density over x∈(m/n,1] yields the displayed formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1, equivalent integral criteria for adjacent comparisons
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n≥2 and let m be an integer with 0≤m≤n−2. With q_m=P(B(n,m/n)≤m), define b_m=((m/n)^(m+1)(1−m/n)^(n−m−1))/(n−m−1). Then q_m≥q_{m+1} if and only if ∫_{m/n}^{(m+1)/n} x^(m+1)(1−x)^(n−m−2) dx ≥ b_m. In addition, for the displayed formulas with 1/m, i.e. for m≥1, this comparison is equivalent to ∫_0^1 (1+t/m)^(m+1)(1−t/(n−m))^(n−m−2) dt ≥ (n−m)/(n−m−1), and also equivalent to ∫_0^1 (1−v/(m+1))^m (1+v/(n−m−1))^(n−m−1) dv ≥ 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Insert the beta-integral representation of q_m and q_{m+1}. After simplifying the binomial coefficients, q_m≥q_{m+1} is equivalent to ((m+1)/(n−m−1)) ∫_{m/n}^1 x^m(1−x)^(n−m−1) dx ≥ ∫_{(m+1)/n}^1 x^(m+1)(1−x)^(n−m−2) dx. Integrating by parts in the left-hand integral transforms the left side into −b_m+∫_{m/n}^1 x^(m+1)(1−x)^(n−m−2) dx, so cancellation with the right side leaves precisely the integral over [m/n,(m+1)/n] bounded below by b_m. For the second form, substitute x=m/n+t/n in that interval and divide by (m/n)^(m+1)(1−m/n)^(n−m−2). For the third form, multiply the second inequality by (n−m−1)/(n−m), integrate by parts on the resulting left-hand side, divide by the endpoint factor (1−1/(n−m))^(n−m−1)(1+1/m)^m, and finally set t=1−v.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2, monotonicity of the normalized comparison integral
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix n≥3. For each v∈(0,1], define, for real x∈[1,n−2], g_v(x)=(1−v/(x+1))^x (1+v/(n−x−1))^(n−x−1). Then g_v is decreasing on [1,n−2]. Consequently h(x)=∫_0^1 (1−v/(x+1))^x (1+v/(n−x−1))^(n−x−1) dv is decreasing on [1,n−2].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Differentiate log g_v(x). The derivative has the sign of log(1−v/(x+1)) + [v x/(x+1)^2]/[1−v/(x+1)] − log(1+v/(n−x−1)) + [v/(n−x−1)]/[1+v/(n−x−1)]. Thus g_v'(x)<0 follows if [v/(n−x−1)]/[1+v/(n−x−1)] < log(1+v/(n−x−1)) and log(1+ (v/(x+1))/(1−v/(x+1))) > [v x/(x+1)^2]/[1−v/(x+1)]. The first inequality is the elementary log inequality c/(1+c)<log(1+c) for c>0. For the second, set c=v/(x+1−v); after algebra it becomes log(1+c)−c+c^2/[v(c+1)]>0. Since v≤1, this left side is at least log(1+c)−c+c^2/(c+1)=log(1+c)−c/(c+1), which is positive again by c/(1+c)<log(1+c). Therefore the derivative is strictly negative for v∈(0,1], and integrating over v gives monotonicity of h.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Boundary estimates, equations (8)-(10)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n=3s+r with s≥1 and r∈{0,1,2}. The proof uses the following boundary inequalities for the decreasing integral h_n(m)=∫_0^1 (1−v/(m+1))^m (1+v/(n−m−1))^(n−m−1) dv. For r=0, h_{3s}(2s−1)=∫_0^1 (1−v/(2s))^(2s−1)(1+v/s)^s dv ≥1, and for s≥2, h_{3s}(2s)=∫_0^1 (1−v/(2s+1))^(2s)(1+v/(s−1))^(s−1) dv <1. For r=1,2, h_{3s+r}(2s)=J_r:=∫_0^1 (1−v/(2s+1))^(2s)(1+v/(s+r−1))^(s+r−1) dv ≥1. Also, for the valid denominator ranges, h_{3s+r}(2s+1)=H_r:=∫_0^1 (1−v/(2s+2))^(2s+1)(1+v/(s+r−2))^(s+r−2) dv <1; the proof reduces this to H_2<1, and H_1≤H_2 when H_1 is meaningful. Small endpoint cases where a displayed denominator would be zero are handled directly in the proof of the theorem.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the lower bound h_{3s}(2s−1)≥1, rewrite the integrand as [(1−v/(2s))^2(1+v/s)]^s (1−v/(2s))^(−1). The bracket equals 1−3v^2/(4s^2)+v^3/(4s^3). Bernoulli's inequality (1+c)^s≥1+sc gives a lower bound by (1−3v^2/(4s)+v^3/(4s^2))(1−v/(2s))^(−1). Using (1−c)^(−1)>1+c+c^2 for c∈(0,1), integration gives 1+5/(96s^2)−1/(80s^3)+1/(96s^4)>1. For the upper bound h_{3s}(2s)<1, define I_s=∫_0^1 (1−v/(2s+1))^(2s)(1+v/(s−1))^(s−1) dv. Write I_s=∫_0^1 [(2s+1)/(2s+1−v)] exp((2s+1)log(1−v/(2s+1))+(s−1)log(1+v/(s−1))) dv. The linear terms in the logarithmic series cancel, giving an exponent Σ_{k≥2} v^k/k [(-1)^(k−1)/(s−1)^(k−1) − 1/(2s+1)^(k−1)]. The tail from k=4 onward is negative, so the exponent is bounded above by γ(s,v), the k=2,3 truncation: γ(s,v)=−3v^2 s/[2(s−1)(2s+1)] + v^3 s(s+2)/[(s−1)^2(2s+1)^2], and γ(s,v)<0 for s≥2 and v∈(0,1]. Then exp(γ)<1+γ+γ^2/2, and (2s+1)/(2s+1−v)≤1+v/(2s+1)+5v^2/[4(2s+1)^2]. Integrating the resulting polynomial gives I_s < 1 + [1+3s(−11s^3+(s+4)^2)]/[(s−1)^4(2s+1)^6] + [s^6(29+7s−15s^2/2)]/[(s−1)^4(2s+1)^6]. Both displayed numerators are negative for s≥3, and I_2<1 is checked by direct computation. For J_r, the inequality (1+v/s)^s≤(1+v/(s+1))^(s+1) implies J_1≤J_2, so it suffices to prove J_1≥1. Rewrite J_1 as ∫_0^1 [(1−v/(2s+1))^2(1+v/s)]^s dv, expand the bracket as 1+v(1−2v)/[s(2s+1)] + [v^2/(2s+1)^2](1+v/s), and apply Bernoulli's inequality. Integration gives J_1≥1−1/[6(2s+1)] + s/[3(2s+1)^2] + 1/[4(2s+1)^2] = 1+1/[12(2s+1)^2]. For H_r, the inequality (1+v/(s−1))^(s−1)≤(1+v/s)^s gives H_1≤H_2, so it suffices to show H_2<1. The proof repeats the logarithmic-series method: H_2=∫_0^1 [(2s+2)/(2s+2−v)] exp((2s+2)log(1−v/(2s+2))+s log(1+v/s)) dv. The exponent has zero linear term and is bounded above by its k=2,3 truncation λ(s,v)=Σ_{k=2}^3 v^k/k [(-1)^(k−1)/s^(k−1) − 1/(2s+2)^(k−1)], with λ(s,v)<0 for s≥2. Use exp(λ)<1+λ+λ^2/2 and (2s+2)/(2s+2−v)<1+v/(2s+2)+6v^2/[5(2s+2)^2]. Polynomial integration yields H_2 < 1 + [−2s^5−9(s^4+s^3)+8s^2+22s+18]/[64s(s+1)^6], which is less than 1 for s≥2; H_2<1 for s=1 is checked directly.

proof_techniques.name: Uniform order-statistic encoding of binomial tails
proof_techniques.description: The paper realizes B(n,m/n) as the count of independent Uniform[0,1] variables below the threshold m/n. The lower tail event B(n,m/n)≤m is then exactly the order-statistic event U_(m+1)>m/n. This converts the binomial CDF at its mean into a beta-density tail integral with explicit density (m+1)binom(n,m+1)x^m(1−x)^(n−m−1).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For equal weights in the original weighted Bernoulli problem, the weighted sum is a normalized binomial variable. This representation can convert equal-weight probability inequalities into beta-integral inequalities where calculus and integration by parts can be applied.

proof_techniques.name: Adjacent-comparison normalization by integration by parts
proof_techniques.description: Instead of estimating q_m directly, the paper compares adjacent probabilities q_m and q_{m+1}. After substituting the beta-integral expressions and simplifying binomial coefficients, an integration by parts collapses the comparison to an integral over the short interval [m/n,(m+1)/n] plus a boundary term. Two changes of variables then normalize the comparison into the clean criterion h_n(m)≥1, where h_n(m)=∫_0^1 (1−v/(m+1))^m(1+v/(n−m−1))^(n−m−1) dv.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original problem is attacked first on rational grid values p=m/n and equal weights, adjacent-comparison formulas may reveal where the worst p values lie without needing absolute tail estimates. Similar normalization may also help compare candidate extremal weight vectors.

proof_techniques.name: Treating the discrete index as continuous to prove monotonicity
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that the normalized comparison integral h_n(m) decreases with m by extending the integrand to real x and differentiating log g_v(x). The derivative is shown negative pointwise in v using only log(1+c)>c/(1+c) and a related inequality obtained after the substitution c=v/(x+1−v). Pointwise monotonicity of the integrand yields monotonicity of the integral.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This technique can reduce a family of finitely many binomial-tail inequalities to checking boundary indices. In the original problem, a comparable continuous-index monotonicity argument might identify extremal equal-weight counterexamples or certify intervals of p.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary reduction by n modulo 3
proof_techniques.description: Once h_n(m) is known to be decreasing, the entire Chvátal-Janson monotonicity pattern follows from two boundary checks. Writing n=3s+r, the cutoff m+1/2<2n/3 translates into checking h_{3s}(2s−1)≥1 and h_{3s}(2s)<1 for r=0, and checking h_{3s+r}(2s)≥1 and h_{3s+r}(2s+1)<1 for r=1,2. This modular decomposition is what turns a global sequence problem into a few explicit integral estimates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For equal weights and rational p, failures or sharp cases may occur at arithmetic boundary locations depending on n mod a small denominator. This paper's n mod 3 cutoff around 2n/3 is a concrete pattern to test against candidate p thresholds.

proof_techniques.name: Bernoulli-inequality lower bounds after algebraic regrouping
proof_techniques.description: For boundary integrals that must be shown at least 1, the paper groups the integrand into powers of a single bracket, such as [(1−v/(2s))^2(1+v/s)]^s, expands the bracket exactly, and applies Bernoulli's inequality (1+c)^s≥1+sc. Remaining factors like (1−v/(2s))^(−1) are bounded below by a short geometric expansion, and the resulting polynomial is integrated exactly.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a template for rigorous finite-n lower bounds on binomial probabilities without asymptotics. It may be useful when trying to prove the original inequality for ranges of p where equal-weight binomial tails are expected to be safely above the target.

proof_techniques.name: Log-series truncation with negative-tail control for upper bounds
proof_techniques.description: For boundary integrals that must be shown below 1, the paper rewrites the product integrand as a simple rational prefactor times an exponential of logarithms. The logarithmic series has cancellation of the linear term, and the tail from degree 4 onward is negative, so the exponent is bounded above by the quadratic-cubic truncation. That truncation is itself negative; the paper then uses exp(c)<1+c+c^2/2 for c<0, bounds the rational prefactor by a quadratic polynomial, and integrates explicitly to get a rational expression less than 1.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a robust finite-sample method for proving strict upper bounds on binomial tail integrals near a delicate threshold. It may help establish equal-weight counterexamples or sharp non-validity ranges in the original problem without relying on normal approximations.

proof_techniques.name: Using monotonicity of a↦(1+v/a)^a to compare residue classes
proof_techniques.description: The proof uses simple comparisons such as (1+v/s)^s≤(1+v/(s+1))^(s+1) and (1+v/(s−1))^(s−1)≤(1+v/s)^s for v≥0. These reduce some boundary estimates for r=1,2 to a single harder case, e.g. J_1≥1 implies J_2≥1 and H_2<1 implies H_1<1.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When equal-weight binomial expressions differ only by neighboring denominator/exponent parameters, this monotonicity can reduce the number of cases needed in a p-classification.

other_useful_info: The paper notes that Janson (2021) had proved the Chvátal conjecture for large n using an Edgeworth expansion for integer-valued central limit probabilities, while this paper gives an elementary all-n proof. It also points out that Rigollet and Tong (2011, Appendix B) used a similar approach to show q_m≥q_{m+1} for 0≤m<n/2 and n≥2. Related references cited for probabilities that a binomial variable exceeds or is at most its expectation include Doerr (2018), Greenberg and Mohri (2014), Jogdeo and Samuels (1968), Pelekis and Ramon (2016), Slud (1977), and Vapnik (1998).
why_relevant: Proves the Chvátal-Janson conjecture for binomial variables with parameter m/n, giving precise control of tails exactly at integer means. This is important for determining which p fail already for equal weights.

title: Nonnegative k-sums, fractional covers, and probability of small deviations
why_relevant: Connects small-deviation probabilities for independent nonnegative sums with weighted subset and fractional-cover arguments. The random-subset viewpoint is very close to interpreting the problem as the p-biased measure of a weighted threshold family.

title: The Markov Inequality for Sums of Independent Random Variables
why_relevant: Develops sharp Markov-type inequalities for sums of independent nonnegative variables, a natural framework for bounding probabilities of weighted Bernoulli sums crossing their mean.

title: On the Number of Successes in Independent Trials
why_relevant: Studies inequalities for sums of independent Bernoulli trials, including non-identical trials. These Poisson-binomial techniques are useful when reducing weighted Bernoulli sums to counts by discretizing or grouping weights.

title: On a Chebyshev-Type Inequality for Sums of Independent Random Variables
why_relevant: This is the original Samuels paper on extremal probabilities for sums of independent nonnegative random variables with prescribed expectations. After scaling, the problem is a two-point special case of this small-deviation/extremal-tail framework.

title: Probability Inequalities for Sums of Bounded Random Variables
why_relevant: Hoeffding's classic paper gives comparison and concentration tools for sums of independent bounded variables. Its convex-order and extremal-distribution techniques are standard for reducing weighted Bernoulli problems to simpler extremal cases.

title: Large matchings in uniform hypergraphs and the conjectures of Erdős and Samuels
why_relevant: Uses special cases of Samuels' conjecture to prove fractional matching results, and contains useful extremal formulations for independent nonnegative variables with two-point extremizers. These techniques may help handle weighted Bernoulli threshold events via hypergraph/fractional-cover reductions.

title: On the probability that a binomial variable is at most its expectation
overall_summary: The paper studies q_m = P(Bi(n,m/n) ≤ m), the probability that a binomial random variable with integer expectation m is at most its mean. Its main result proves, for all sufficiently large n, Chvátal's conjecture that q_m is minimized when m is the integer closest to 2n/3, and in fact proves an inverse-unimodality statement for the consecutive differences q_{m+1} − q_m. The core method is a uniform version of Esseen's lattice Edgeworth expansion, including periodic continuity-correction terms, specialized to Bernoulli variables and then combined with Poisson approximation near the endpoints m ≈ 0 and m ≈ n. For the original weighted Bernoulli problem, the paper is directly useful in the equal-weight case and as a guide to the discontinuities and fine lattice effects that occur when the threshold equals the expectation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Neumann median inequality for binomial variables, quoted as equation (1.1)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X have the binomial distribution Bi(n,p), and suppose np = m is an integer with m in {0,...,n}. Then P(X < np) < 1/2 < P(X ≤ np). Equivalently, when the mean of a binomial variable is an integer, that mean is a strong median.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not reprove this result; it cites Neumann (1966), with other proofs by Jogdeo and Samuels, Kaas and Buhrman, and Knuth. The paper immediately uses it to explain the shape of p ↦ P(Bi(n,p) ≤ np): for fixed n the function oscillates around 1/2, has upward jumps at p = m/n, and is monotone decreasing between consecutive jumps. This observation motivates focusing on the integer-mean values q_m = P(Bi(n,m/n) ≤ m), which are the local maxima of the oscillating function.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2, asymptotic Chvátal conjecture and inverse unimodality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each n ≥ 2 and each integer m in {0,...,n}, define q_m = P(Bi(n,m/n) ≤ m). There exists n_0 such that for every n ≥ n_0, q_m is smallest when m is the integer closest to 2n/3. Moreover, for n ≥ n_0 and m = 0,...,n−1, the difference q_{m+1} − q_m is negative when m + 1/2 < 2n/3 and positive when m + 1/2 > 2n/3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write p_m = m/n. The proof is by estimating q_{m+1} − q_m in several ranges. In the central range 2 log^2 n ≤ m ≤ n − 2 log^2 n, the Bernoulli Edgeworth expansion gives q_m = 1/2 + h_1(p_m)n^{-1/2} + h_3(p_m)n^{-3/2} + O((n p_m(1−p_m))^{-5/2}), where h_1(p) = (2−p)/(3 sqrt(2π p(1−p))) and h_3(p) = (2−p)(p^2+23p−23)/(540 sqrt(2π)(p(1−p))^{3/2}). Differentiating gives h_1'(p) = (3p−2)/(6 sqrt(2π)(p(1−p))^{3/2}), while h_3'(p) = O((p(1−p))^{-5/2}). Subtracting the expansions for p_m and p_{m+1}, and using the mean value theorem, yields q_{m+1} − q_m = h_1'(p'_m)n^{-3/2} + h_3'(p''_m)n^{-5/2} + O((n p_m(1−p_m))^{-5/2}) for some p'_m,p''_m in [p_m,p_{m+1}]. Thus the sign is controlled by 3p_m−2 except in a bounded window around p = 2/3. This immediately gives negativity for 2 log^2 n ≤ m ≤ n/2, negativity for n/2 ≤ m ≤ 2n/3 − C_1 with C_1 large, and positivity for 2n/3 + C_2 ≤ m ≤ n − 2 log^2 n with C_2 large. In the delicate bounded window 2n/3 − C_1 ≤ m ≤ 2n/3 + C_2, the proof uses the Edgeworth expansion two orders further: q_m = 1/2 + h_1(p_m)n^{-1/2} + h_3(p_m)n^{-3/2} + h_5(p_m)n^{-5/2} + O(n^{-7/2}). Taylor expanding at p = 2/3, using h_1'(2/3)=0, gives q_m = G(n) + H(m−2n/3)n^{-5/2} + O(n^{-7/2}), where H(x) = (1/2)h_1''(2/3)x^2 + h_3'(2/3)x. The computed constants are h_1''(2/3) = 27/(8 sqrt π) and h_3'(2/3) = 9/(40 sqrt π) = h_1''(2/3)/15. Therefore q_{m+1} − q_m = [h_1''(2/3)(m−2n/3+1/2) + h_3'(2/3)]n^{-5/2} + O(n^{-7/2}), and the ratio 1/15 < 1/6 gives the required sign on the two sides of the closest integer to 2n/3. For the lower endpoint m < 2 log^2 n, the paper invokes Rigollet and Tong's monotonicity result, whose proof gives q_{m−1} > q_m for m ≤ n/2. For the upper endpoint m > n − 2 log^2 n, the proof uses symmetry and defines q'_m = P(Bi(n,m/n) < m); since 1 − q_m = q'_{n−m}, it suffices to prove q'_{m−1} < q'_m for small m. This is done by total variation Poisson approximation d_TV(Bi(n,m/n),Po(m)) < m/n, the Edgeworth expansion P(Po(m) < m) = 1/2 − (3 sqrt(2π))^{-1}m^{-1/2} − 23(270 sqrt(2π))^{-1}m^{-3/2} + O(m^{-5/2}), and Lemma 6.1 for bounded m.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1, Esseen lattice Edgeworth expansion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X, X_1, X_2,... be independent identically distributed integer-valued random variables of span 1, let S_n = X_1 + ... + X_n, let μ = E X, σ^2 = Var X, and let γ_j be the cumulants of X. Put λ_j = γ_j/σ^j. For j ≥ 1 define polynomials P_j(u) by the formal identity exp(∑_{i≥1} λ_{i+2} u^{i+2} z^i/(i+2)!) = 1 + ∑_{j≥1} P_j(u) z^j. If P_j(u) = ∑_r ϖ_{jr}u^r, define Q_j(x) = ∑_r (−1)^r ϖ_{jr} Φ^{(r)}(x), where Φ is the standard normal distribution function. Define ψ_1(x) = 1/2 − {x}, made right-continuous at integers, and for ℓ ≥ 2 define ψ_ℓ(x) = ∑_{k in Z, k ≠ 0} exp(2πikx)/(2πik)^ℓ. For an integer k ≥ 1, assume E|X|^{k+2} < ∞ and define Ψ_{n,k}(x) = Φ(x) + ∑_{j=1}^k n^{-j/2}Q_j(x) and Ψ^*_{n,k}(x) = Ψ_{n,k}(x) + ∑_{ℓ=1}^k (−1)^{ℓ−1}n^{-ℓ/2}σ^{-ℓ}ψ_ℓ(nμ + xσ sqrt n) Ψ_{n,k}^{(ℓ)}(x). Then P(S_n ≤ nμ + xσ sqrt n) = Ψ^*_{n,k}(x) + R_{n,k}(x), with sup_x |R_{n,k}(x)| = o(n^{-k/2}) as n → ∞. If in addition E|X|^{k+3} < ∞, then sup_x |R_{n,k}(x)| = O(n^{-(k+1)/2}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This theorem is quoted from Esseen (1945), not reproved in full. The paper notes that the stated O(n^{-(k+1)/2}) version follows immediately by applying Esseen's o(n^{-k/2}) expansion with k replaced by k+1 and then truncating. The terms involving ψ_ℓ are the lattice continuity corrections; the right-continuous convention for ψ_1 is used so that the expansion is valid at lattice points as a right-continuous distribution function.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2, uniform Edgeworth expansion under an adjacent-mass condition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix an integer k ≥ 1 and a constant c > 0. There exists C, depending only on k and c, such that the following holds. Let X be an integer-valued random variable with E|X|^{k+3} < ∞, mean μ, variance σ^2, and Λ_j = E|X−μ|^j/σ^j. Assume min{P(X=0), P(X=1)} ≥ cσ^2. Let n ≥ 2 be an integer satisfying σ sqrt n ≥ log n. For S_n = X_1 + ... + X_n with X_i independent copies of X, the Esseen expansion P(S_n ≤ nμ + xσ sqrt n) = Ψ^*_{n,k}(x) + R_{n,k}(x), with Ψ^*_{n,k} as in Theorem 3.1, holds uniformly with sup_x |R_{n,k}(x)| ≤ C Λ_{k+3} n^{-(k+1)/2} ≤ C E|X|^{k+3} σ^{-(k+3)} n^{-(k+1)/2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof follows Esseen's Fourier-analytic proof but tracks constants uniformly over the distribution of X. Define f(t)=E exp(it(X−μ)), f_n(t)=f(t/(σ sqrt n))^n, and g_0(t)=exp(−t^2/2)+∑_{j=1}^k n^{-j/2}P_j(it)exp(−t^2/2). Set T = n^{(k+1)/2} and T_1 = sqrt n / Λ_{k+3}^{1/(k+1)}. If Λ_{k+3}n^{-(k+1)/2} is not small, the desired estimate is trivial using Lemma 4.1, so the proof assumes it is small. For |t| ≤ T_1, Petrov's version of the characteristic-function Edgeworth estimate gives |f_n(t) − g_0(t)| ≤ CΛ_{k+3}|t|^{k+3}exp(−t^2/13)n^{-(k+1)/2}, hence ∫_{−T_1}^{T_1}|f_n(t)−g_0(t)|/|t| dt ≤ CΛ_{k+3}n^{-(k+1)/2}. For larger |t| up to πσ sqrt n, Lemma 4.2 and the adjacent-mass assumption give |f_n(t)| ≤ exp(−ct^2), so the tails ∫_{T_1}^{πσ sqrt n}|f_n(t)|/|t| dt and the corresponding integral for g_0 are bounded by C exp(−cT_1^2), which is at most CΛ_{k+3}n^{-(k+1)/2}. The same argument without the factor 1/|t| gives ∫_{−πσ sqrt n}^{πσ sqrt n}|f_n(t)−g_0(t)| dt ≤ CΛ_{k+3}n^{-(k+1)/2}. Esseen's lattice inversion then produces additional shifted-denominator terms with denominators |t+2πjσ sqrt n|. These are bounded by C(|j|σ sqrt n)^{-1}Λ_{k+3}n^{-(k+1)/2}; summing over the relevant j contributes a factor log n/(σ sqrt n), which is absorbed exactly by the hypothesis σ sqrt n ≥ log n. The remaining parts are handled as in Esseen's proof.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 3.4, reduced continuity-correction expansion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of Theorem 3.1 or Theorem 3.2, the expansion may be written with redundant higher-order products removed. Define tilde Ψ^*_{n,k}(x) = Ψ_{n,k}(x) + ∑_{ℓ=1}^k (−1)^{ℓ−1}n^{-ℓ/2}σ^{-ℓ}ψ_ℓ(nμ + xσ sqrt n) Ψ_{n,k−ℓ}^{(ℓ)}(x). Then P(S_n ≤ nμ + xσ sqrt n) = tilde Ψ^*_{n,k}(x) + tilde R_{n,k}(x), and the same remainder bounds as in Theorem 3.1 and Theorem 3.2 hold.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The full Ψ^*_{n,k} contains terms whose total order is beyond n^{-k/2}. Removing them changes the expansion only by terms of order O(n^{-m/2}) with m ≥ k+1. For Theorem 3.2, Lemma 4.1 bounds the removed terms by the same quantity Λ_{k+3}n^{-(k+1)/2}, using also the lower bound implicit in σ sqrt n ≥ log n and the fact that for integer-valued X, lower moments are controlled by higher moments. This reduced form is used in Section 5 to evaluate the expansion at x=0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1, uniform bounds for Edgeworth coefficients and derivatives
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ℓ ≥ 1 and suppose E|X|^{ℓ+2} < ∞. With Λ_j = E|X−μ|^j/σ^j and with P_ℓ(u), Q_ℓ(x), and Ψ_{n,ℓ}(x) defined as in the Edgeworth expansion, for every derivative order m ≥ 0 there are constants C_ℓ and C_{ℓ,m} such that |ϖ_{ℓ,r}| ≤ C_ℓΛ_{ℓ+2}, |P_ℓ(u)| ≤ C_ℓΛ_{ℓ+2}(|u|^{ℓ+2}+|u|^{3ℓ}), |Q_ℓ^{(m)}(x)| ≤ C_{ℓ,m}Λ_{ℓ+2}, and |Ψ_{n,ℓ}^{(m)}(x)| ≤ C_{ℓ,m}(1+Λ_{ℓ+2}n^{-ℓ/2}) for all relevant r,u,x,n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: From the formal definition of P_ℓ, each coefficient ϖ_{ℓ,r} is a linear combination of products ∏_{s=1}^a λ_{i_s+2} with ∑_s i_s = ℓ and ∑_s(i_s+2)=r. Cumulants satisfy |λ_j| ≤ C_jΛ_j, and Hölder's inequality gives Λ_{i_s+2} ≤ Λ_{ℓ+2}^{i_s/ℓ}. Thus each product is at most C_ℓΛ_{ℓ+2}, proving the coefficient bound. The polynomial bound for P_ℓ follows from the degree range j+2 ≤ r ≤ 3j. The derivative bound for Q_ℓ follows because Φ and all its derivatives are bounded on R. Finally Ψ_{n,ℓ}^{(m)} is Φ^{(m)} plus a finite sum n^{-j/2}Q_j^{(m)}; applying the previous bounds and Hölder again gives the displayed estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2, characteristic-function damping from two adjacent atoms
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If X is a random variable satisfying P(X=0) ≥ a and P(X=1) ≥ a for some a ≥ 0, then for all |t| ≤ π, |E exp(itX)| ≤ 1 − aπ^{-2}t^2 ≤ exp(−aπ^{-2}t^2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Subtract the masses at 0 and 1: E exp(itX) − a − a exp(it) is the Fourier transform of a positive measure of total mass 1−2a. Therefore |E exp(itX)| ≤ a|1+exp(it)| + 1−2a = 2a|cos(t/2)| + 1−2a. For |t| ≤ π this equals 1 − 2a(1−cos(t/2)) = 1 − 4a sin^2(t/4), and the elementary lower bound on sin on [0,π/4] gives 1 − aπ^{-2}t^2. The exponential bound follows from 1−y ≤ exp(−y).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equations (5.1) and (5.2), Edgeworth expansion evaluated at the mean
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of the reduced expansion, taking x=0 gives P(S_n ≤ nμ) = Ψ_{n,k}(0) + ∑_{ℓ=1}^k (−1)^{ℓ−1}n^{-ℓ/2}σ^{-ℓ}ψ_ℓ(nμ) Ψ_{n,k−ℓ}^{(ℓ)}(0) + tilde R_{n,k}(0). If additionally nμ is an integer, ψ_ℓ(nμ)=ψ_ℓ(0)=−B_ℓ/ℓ!, where B_ℓ are Bernoulli numbers, and with Q_0=Φ this becomes P(S_n ≤ nμ) = 1/2 + ∑_{s=1}^k n^{-s/2} ∑_{ℓ=0}^s σ^{-ℓ}(−1)^ℓ B_ℓ Q_{s−ℓ}^{(ℓ)}(0)/ℓ! + tilde R_{n,k}(0). All terms with even s vanish.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first formula is just the reduced Edgeworth expansion at x=0. When nμ is an integer, the periodic functions ψ_ℓ are evaluated at 0; the paper uses ψ_ℓ(0)=−B_ℓ/ℓ!, with B_1=−1/2, B_2=1/6, and B_{2j+1}=0 for j ≥ 1. Reindexing the double sum by total order s=j+ℓ gives the displayed expansion. The parity cancellation follows because φ is even, so its odd derivatives at 0 vanish, and because the coefficients ϖ_{jr} in Q_j satisfy ϖ_{jr}=0 unless r−j is even. Therefore Q_j^{(ℓ)}(0)=0 whenever j and ℓ have the same parity, except for j=ℓ=0; this kills every even total order s. Remark 5.1 adds that for P(S_n < nμ), the same formulas hold except that the sign is changed for the terms with ℓ=1, because ψ_1 must be taken left-continuously.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 1.4 and equation (1.2), first-order expansion for arbitrary p
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For general p, at least under the condition n p(1−p) ≥ log^2 n, the paper states the asymptotic formula P(Bi(n,p) ≤ np) = 1/2 + [4 − 2p − 6{np}]/[6 sqrt(2π n p(1−p))] + O(1/(n p(1−p))). Here {np} denotes the fractional part of np.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is stated as a consequence of Theorem 3.2 and equation (5.1) with k=1. For X distributed as Bernoulli(p), the normalized third cumulant contributes the ordinary first Edgeworth correction Q_1(0), and the lattice correction contributes a term involving ψ_1(np)=1/2−{np}. Combining these two first-order terms gives the numerator 4−2p−6{np}. The theorem's uniform error bound gives the stated O(1/(n p(1−p))) remainder under n p(1−p) ≥ log^2 n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equations (5.6) to (5.8), binomial expansion at an integer mean through order n^{-3/2}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S_n have the binomial distribution Bi(n,p), with 0 < p < 1, and suppose np is an integer. If sqrt(n p(1−p)) ≥ log n, then P(S_n ≤ np) = 1/2 + h_1(p)n^{-1/2} + h_3(p)n^{-3/2} + O((n p(1−p))^{-5/2}), where h_1(p) = (2−p)/(3 sqrt(2π p(1−p))) and h_3(p) = (2−p)(p^2+23p−23)/(540 sqrt(2π)(p(1−p))^{3/2}). The terms of orders n^{-1} and n^{-2} vanish.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For X distributed as Bernoulli(p), σ^2=p(1−p) and all moments exist. Also E|X−p|^j ≤ σ^2, so Λ_j ≤ σ^{2−j}, and min{P(X=0),P(X=1)} ≥ σ^2; hence Theorem 3.2 applies with c=1 whenever sqrt(n p(1−p)) ≥ log n and gives remainders O((n p(1−p))^{-(k+1)/2}). The integer-mean formula (5.2) is then applied with k=4. Computing the Bernoulli cumulants γ_3, γ_4, and γ_5 and substituting them into Q_1, Q_2, and Q_3 yields the displayed h_1 and h_3. The absence of h_2 and h_4 is exactly the parity cancellation in the integer-mean Edgeworth expansion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Derivative estimates used in Section 6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For h_1 and h_3 defined in the binomial integer-mean expansion, h_1'(p) = (3p−2)/(6 sqrt(2π)(p(1−p))^{3/2}), so h_1 decreases on (0,2/3), increases on (2/3,1), and has its unique critical point at p=2/3. Also h_3'(p)=O((p(1−p))^{-5/2}). At p=2/3 the higher constants used in the critical window are h_1''(2/3)=27/(8 sqrt π) and h_3'(2/3)=9/(40 sqrt π)=h_1''(2/3)/15.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The formula for h_1' follows by differentiating h_1(p)=(2−p)/(3 sqrt(2π p(1−p))). The bound on h_3' follows directly from the explicit rational expression for h_3(p), since differentiating increases the endpoint singularity by one power of p(1−p). In the bounded critical window near 2/3, the paper differentiates explicitly again to obtain h_1''(2/3) and h_3'(2/3). These constants determine the sign of q_{m+1}−q_m when the leading derivative h_1' is of order 1/n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1, monotonicity of Poisson probabilities at the mean
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every integer m ≥ 0, P(Po(m) < m) < P(Po(m+1) < m+1) < 1/2, and P(Po(m) ≤ m) > P(Po(m+1) ≤ m+1) > 1/2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Consider a Poisson process of rate 1 on (0,∞), and let T_1<T_2<... be its arrival times. The number of points in [0,m] has distribution Po(m), so P(Po(m)<m)=P(T_m>m)=∫_m^∞ t^{m−1}e^{−t}/(m−1)! dt. Subtracting the analogous formula for m+1 gives P(Po(m)<m) − P(Po(m+1)<m+1) = ∫_m^{m+1}[t^m e^{−t} − m^m e^{−m}]/m! dt, which is negative because t^m e^{−t} decreases for t ≥ m. Similarly, P(Po(m)≤m) − P(Po(m+1)≤m+1) = ∫_m^{m+1}[t^{m+1}e^{−t} − m^{m+1}e^{−m}]/(m+1)! dt, which is positive because t^{m+1}e^{−t} increases for t ≤ m+1. The inequalities relative to 1/2 follow from these monotonicities together with the normal approximation limit 1/2 as m → ∞.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Endpoint Poisson approximation estimates, equations (6.14) to (6.16)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For q'_m = P(Bi(n,m/n) < m), the paper uses the standard total variation bound d_TV(Bi(n,p),Po(np)) < p to get |q'_m − P(Po(m)<m)| < m/n. It also uses the expansion P(Po(m)<m) = 1/2 − [1/(3 sqrt(2π))]m^{-1/2} − [23/(270 sqrt(2π))]m^{-3/2} + O(m^{-5/2}). Consequently, for m ≥ 2, q'_m − q'_{m−1} = [1/(6 sqrt(2π))]m^{-3/2} + O(m^{-5/2}) + O(m/n), which is positive for C_3 < m < 2 log^2 n and n sufficiently large, with C_3 fixed large enough.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The total variation estimate is cited from Barbour, Holst, and Janson's Poisson approximation theorem. The Poisson expansion is obtained by applying Theorem 3.2, or the classical Esseen theorem, to a sum of m independent Poisson(1) variables and using the strict-inequality version from Remark 5.1, which changes the sign of the ℓ=1 continuity-correction terms. Subtracting consecutive values of the expansion gives the m^{-3/2} leading difference. The approximation error contributes O(m/n), which is negligible in the endpoint range m < 2 log^2 n after C_3 is fixed and n is large. For bounded m ≤ C_3, the strict monotonicity is supplied directly by Lemma 6.1 and the same total variation approximation.

proof_techniques.name: Lattice Edgeworth expansion with periodic continuity corrections
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses Esseen's expansion for integer-valued sums, not just the continuous Edgeworth expansion. The correction terms involve periodic functions ψ_ℓ(nμ + xσ sqrt n), and these terms encode the discontinuities and fractional-part effects of a lattice distribution. The convention ψ_1(x)=1/2−{x}, right-continuously at integers, is important because the distribution function is right-continuous and the expansion is evaluated exactly at lattice points.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem contains exact threshold events for Bernoulli sums, where lattice jumps are often decisive. In the equal-weight case, this technique directly analyzes P(Bi(m,p) ≥ mp) or its complementary lower-tail forms at and near the expectation.

proof_techniques.name: Uniformizing Edgeworth estimates by controlling the characteristic function away from zero
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves a uniform version of Esseen's expansion by requiring a fixed lower bound, relative to variance, on P(X=0) and P(X=1). Lemma 4.2 then gives a Gaussian-type decay bound |E exp(itX)| ≤ exp(−ct^2) for |t|≤π after normalization, preventing the lattice span from degenerating and allowing the Fourier inversion errors to be controlled uniformly.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Bernoulli variables and many Bernoulli-derived lattice variables have adjacent atoms, so this mechanism can be reused to obtain uniform asymptotics across ranges of p. For more general weighted sums, an analogue would be needed to rule out near-periodic or coarse-lattice obstructions.

proof_techniques.name: Evaluation at an integer mean using Bernoulli numbers
proof_techniques.description: At x=0 and nμ integer, all periodic corrections are evaluated at zero, where ψ_ℓ(0)=−B_ℓ/ℓ!. This transforms the Edgeworth expansion into an explicit algebraic series involving derivatives Q_j^{(ℓ)}(0). Because the normal density is even and the Edgeworth polynomials have a parity constraint, all even total orders vanish in the integer-mean expansion.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When a weighted Bernoulli sum has a threshold exactly at its mean and a lattice structure, this kind of simplification can identify the first nonzero correction to 1/2. It is especially relevant for equal weights or rational weights after clearing denominators.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-difference sign from the derivative of the leading correction
proof_techniques.description: For q_m=P(Bi(n,m/n)≤m), the leading correction is h_1(m/n)n^{-1/2}. Since consecutive m values change p by 1/n, q_{m+1}−q_m is controlled to first order by h_1'(m/n)n^{-3/2}. The sign change h_1'(p) ∝ 3p−2 is the main reason the minimum occurs near p=2/3.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For identifying exceptional p-values in the original problem, one can look for where the leading asymptotic correction changes sign in the equal-weight model. This gives candidate thresholds or worst cases before attempting a general weighted proof.

proof_techniques.name: Higher-order expansion in a critical window
proof_techniques.description: Where the derivative of the leading correction vanishes, the paper expands to two more Edgeworth orders and then Taylor-expands around the critical point p=2/3. This reduces q_m near the minimum to G(n)+H(m−2n/3)n^{-5/2}+O(n^{-7/2}), with an explicit quadratic-linear polynomial H. The exact constants h_1''(2/3) and h_3'(2/3) then decide the sign of q_{m+1}−q_m at the integer scale.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original problem has borderline p values where a first-order asymptotic is inconclusive, this illustrates how to resolve the lattice-scale question by keeping additional Edgeworth terms rather than relying only on the central limit theorem.

proof_techniques.name: Endpoint reduction to Poisson approximation
proof_techniques.description: The uniform normal/Edgeworth expansion used in the central range requires n p(1−p) large. Near p=0 or p=1, the paper switches to Poisson approximation: Bi(n,m/n) is close in total variation to Po(m) with error less than m/n. Monotonicity and a separate Poisson Edgeworth expansion then control the endpoint consecutive differences.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For weighted Bernoulli sums with very small or very large p, Poisson or compound-Poisson approximations may replace normal approximations. This is a useful way to handle sparse regimes that are invisible to a variance-large analysis.

proof_techniques.name: Symmetry between lower and upper binomial tails
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the identity that n−Bi(n,p) has distribution Bi(n,1−p). In Theorem 1.2, this converts the upper endpoint m≈n for q_m into a small-parameter problem for strict lower tails q'_m=P(Bi(n,m/n)<m). Remark 1.3 also notes that the maximizer of P(Bi(n,m/n)<m) is near n/3 by symmetry from the minimizer near 2n/3.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original event is an upper-tail event, while the paper mainly studies lower tails. Complementing Bernoulli variables converts weighted upper-tail questions into lower-tail questions for success probability 1−p, with a corresponding change in the threshold.

proof_techniques.name: Strong-median and oscillation viewpoint
proof_techniques.description: The quoted Neumann theorem shows that an integer binomial mean is a strict median: the lower strict tail is below 1/2 and the lower weak tail is above 1/2. Consequently, p ↦ P(Bi(n,p)≤np) does not vary smoothly around 1/2 but instead decreases between jumps and jumps upward at rational points m/n.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This warns that proving inequalities at the expectation cannot ignore whether the threshold is attained as a lattice point. In equal-weight Bernoulli sums, the distinction between ≥, >, ≤, and < at mp integer can change the extremal behavior.

other_useful_info: Conjecture 1.1, due to Chvátal, asserts that for every fixed n ≥ 2, q_m=P(Bi(n,m/n)≤m) is minimized at the integer m closest to 2n/3; the paper proves this only for sufficiently large n and reports numerical verification for n ≤ 1000. The paper also conjectures that the inverse-unimodality statement for q_m should hold for all n, while noting that Rigollet and Tong (2011) proved for every n that q_m decreases for m ≤ n/2, and their proof gives strict decrease in the range used here. Remark 1.3 states that, by symmetry, for large n the probability P(Bi(n,m/n)<m) is largest when m is the integer closest to n/3. Remark 1.5 says the constants in the proof could in principle be made explicit to obtain an explicit n_0, but the author does not regard that as practical. The paper points to explicit related binomial-tail bounds by Doerr (2018), Greenberg and Mohri (2014), Pelekis and Ramon (2016), Rigollet and Tong (2011), and Slud (1977), and to Brown, Cai, and DasGupta (2002) for another use of multi-term Edgeworth expansions in binomial oscillation phenomena.
why_relevant: Analyzes binomial distribution functions at the mean and formulates the Chvátal-Janson conjecture about the case p=m/n. This is directly relevant to the discontinuities that occur in the equal-weight version of the problem.

title: Probabilistic Bounds on the Coefficients of Polynomials with Only Real Zeros
overall_summary: Pitman reviews finite nonnegative coefficient sequences whose generating polynomials have only real zeros, equivalently Pólya frequency sequences, and emphasizes Lévy's probabilistic representation of such normalized sequences as distributions of sums of independent Bernoulli variables. The paper collects probabilistic estimates for these Poisson-binomial distributions: Hoeffding comparison with binomial laws, Chernoff-type tail bounds, Darroch's localization of the mode near the mean, coefficient ratio bounds via exponential tilting, and normal and Poisson approximation bounds. Its core technique is to convert real-rootedness or total positivity into an independent-trials representation and then import sharp probability inequalities. For the original weighted-Bernoulli threshold problem, the paper is most useful if one can reduce or compare the weighted sum event to a count distribution or to coefficients of a real-rooted polynomial; then the binomial extremality, mode/median vicinity of the mean, and tail estimates become available.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1: real-rooted coefficients, PF sequences, and Poisson-binomial laws
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (a_0,...,a_n) be nonnegative real numbers, let A(z)=∑_{k=0}^n a_k z^k, and assume A(1)>0. The following are equivalent: (i) A(z) is constant or has only real zeros; (ii) (a_0,...,a_n) is a Pólya frequency sequence, meaning that the infinite Toeplitz matrix (a_{i-j})_{i,j≥0}, with a_r=0 outside {0,...,n}, is totally positive of every order; (iii) the normalized sequence (a_0/A(1),...,a_n/A(1)) is the distribution of S_n=X_1+...+X_n for independent Bernoulli variables X_i with success probabilities q_i in [0,1]. In this representation, the roots of A(z) are -(1-q_i)/q_i for those i with q_i>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence of real-rootedness and the PF property is cited as the Aissen-Schoenberg-Whitney theorem. The equivalence with the Bernoulli-sum representation is described as due to P. Lévy and follows directly from probability generating functions. If A has nonnegative coefficients and only real zeros, its nonzero roots must be nonpositive, so after normalizing by A(1) it factors into linear probability-generating factors of the form (1-q_i)+q_i z, with possible q_i=0 or q_i=1 for degenerate trials. The product of these factors is exactly the probability generating function of a sum of independent Bernoulli(q_i) variables. Conversely, the generating function of such a Bernoulli sum is a product of linear factors, hence has only real zeros.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Newton inequality and log-concavity consequences
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If A(z)=∑_{k=0}^n a_k z^k is a real polynomial with only real zeros, then for every 1≤k≤n-1, a_k^2 ≥ a_{k-1} a_{k+1} (1+1/k)(1+1/(n-k)). In particular, a nonnegative PF sequence is log-concave. The paper also recalls Schoenberg's characterization that a nonnegative sequence is PF of order 2 if and only if it is log-concave and has no internal zeros. Lévy observed that the displayed Newton inequality cannot be improved in the following local sense: given nonnegative a_{k-1}, a_k, a_{k+1} satisfying the inequality for some 1≤k≤n, there exists a PF sequence having these three values in those positions. Samuels further showed that applications of Newton's inequality imply that, for each r≥1, the r-th order difference sequence derived from a finite PF sequence has at most r strict sign changes.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Newton's classical inequality, as recorded for example in Hardy-Littlewood-Pólya, and does not reproduce its proof. In the paper's usage, the inequality is applied to coefficient sequences known to be PF by Proposition 1, hence known to arise from real-rooted generating polynomials. The log-concavity conclusion follows by dropping the extra factor (1+1/k)(1+1/(n-k))≥1. The sharpness statement is attributed to Lévy, and the sign-change statement to Samuels; these are included as consequences rather than proved in the paper.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Mean and variance identities for normalized PF coefficients
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (a_0,...,a_n) be a nonnegative frequency sequence with A(z)=∑_{k=0}^n a_k z^k and A=A(1)>0. Define P(k)=a_k/A for k=0,...,n. Its mean μ and variance σ^2 are μ=A'(1)/A(1) and σ^2=A''(1)/A(1)+A'(1)/A(1)-[A'(1)/A(1)]^2. If (a_0,...,a_n) is PF and P is represented as the law of S_n=∑_{i=1}^n X_i with independent Bernoulli(q_i) variables, then μ=∑_{i=1}^n q_i and σ^2=∑_{i=1}^n q_i(1-q_i).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first two formulas are obtained by differentiating the probability generating function A(z)/A(1) at z=1: the first derivative gives E S, while the second derivative gives E[S(S-1)], from which the variance formula follows. Under the PF assumption, Proposition 1 factors A(z)/A(1) as ∏_i((1-q_i)+q_i z). Differentiating the logarithm of this product, or simply using independence of the Bernoulli summands, gives μ=∑ q_i and σ^2=∑ q_i(1-q_i).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Hoeffding inequalities for PF distributions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a PF distribution on {0,1,...,n} with mean μ. Let P_{n,p} denote the binomial distribution with parameters n and p=μ/n, so P_{n,p} has the same mean μ. Then for all integers b,d satisfying 0≤b≤μ-1 and μ+1≤d≤n, one has P[0,b]≤P_{n,p}[0,b] and P[d,n]≤P_{n,p}[d,n], where P[u,v]=∑_{u≤j≤v} P(j). Also, for every convex function g on {0,1,...,n}, ∑_{j=0}^n g(j)P(j)≤∑_{j=0}^n g(j)P_{n,p}(j). The paper also records Hoeffding's more general extremal statement: for an arbitrary real-valued function g, any PF distribution on {0,...,n} maximizing ∑ g(j)P(j) over all PF distributions on {0,...,n} must be a shifted binomial distribution, i.e. P(k)=P_{m,p}(k-h) on h≤k≤h+m for some integers h,m and some p in [0,1].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Hoeffding's theorem rather than proving it. Through Proposition 1, a PF distribution is the law of a sum of independent Bernoulli variables with possibly unequal success probabilities q_i and fixed total mean μ=∑ q_i. Hoeffding's result says that among all such independent-trial sums with n trials and fixed mean, the equal-probability binomial law with q_i=μ/n is the most spread out in convex order and has maximal upper and lower tails away from the mean. The shifted-binomial extremal statement is a further part of Hoeffding's analysis for arbitrary objective functions g.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Chernoff-Okamoto large-deviation bounds for PF distributions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a PF distribution on {0,1,...,n} with mean μ. For every integer b with μ+1≤b≤n, P[b,n]≤(μ/b)^b ((n-μ)/(n-b))^{n-b}, with the usual convention for a zero exponent. By applying the same estimate to the reversed distribution, the same expression gives an upper bound on P[0,b] for 0≤b≤μ-1. The paper also records simpler bounds, valid for all c>0, in the form P[0,μ-c]≤exp(-c^2/(2μ)) and P[μ+c,n]≤exp(-c^2/(2μ)+c^3/(2μ^2)), where intervals with noninteger endpoints are interpreted by summing over the integer points they contain.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first displayed estimate is obtained in the paper by combining Hoeffding's tail domination with Okamoto's Chernoff-method bound for binomial tail probabilities. Since Hoeffding shows that a PF law with mean μ has upper tail no larger than the binomial(n, μ/n) upper tail beyond μ, the binomial Chernoff estimate transfers directly. The lower-tail estimate follows by reversing the sequence, which changes S to n-S and preserves the PF property. The simpler exponential estimates are quoted from Appendix A of Alon-Spencer and are presented as adequate rough bounds for many purposes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Nicholas Bernoulli interval bound for PF2 distributions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (a_k) be a summable PF2 sequence indexed by integers, let A=∑_k a_k, and let P(k)=a_k/A be the normalized probability distribution. For integers b≤m≤c with a_m>0, P[b,c]≥1-max(a_b,a_c)/a_m. Thus, if b,c,m are chosen so that max(a_b,a_c)/a_m≤ε, then the probability outside [b,c] is at most ε. The paper notes that probabilities P[i,j] with b≤i≤j≤c can then be approximated from above by ∑_{i≤k≤j} a_k / ∑_{b≤k≤c} a_k with relative error at most ε.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as a generalization of a bound discovered by Nicholas Bernoulli for binomial probabilities and does not include a proof. The structural input is that PF2 is equivalent to log-concavity with no internal zeros, so ratios of consecutive coefficients are monotone and tail terms are controlled by endpoint-to-central coefficient ratios. The stated inequality is useful precisely when a_m is chosen near a mode, so both endpoint coefficients are smaller than the central coefficient.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Darroch's rule for the mode of a PF distribution
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (a_0,...,a_n) be a PF sequence and let P(k)=a_k/∑_j a_j have mean μ. The sequence has either a unique mode or two consecutive modes. Darroch's rule says that any mode m satisfies |m-μ|<1. More precisely, for an integer k with 0≤k≤n, Darroch's Theorem 4 gives: if k≤μ<k+1/(k+2), then the mode is m=k; if k+1/(k+2)≤μ≤k+1-1/(n-k+1), then the modes are k, or k+1, or both; if k+1-1/(n-k+1)<μ≤k+1, then the mode is m=k+1, with empty boundary cases interpreted in the obvious way.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Darroch's theorem and uses it as an input. The PF hypothesis is converted by Proposition 1 into the distribution of the number of successes in independent trials, and Darroch's theorem applies to precisely this class of Poisson-binomial distributions. The weaker but often decisive conclusion |m-μ|<1 follows immediately from the three displayed alternatives.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Exponential tilting and consecutive-ratio bounds
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (a_0,...,a_n) be a PF sequence with A(z)=∑_{k=0}^n a_k z^k. For θ>0 define the tilted sequence (a_k θ^k). Its normalized mean and variance are μ(θ)=θ A'(θ)/A(θ) and σ^2(θ)=θ^2 A''(θ)/A(θ)+θ A'(θ)/A(θ)-[θ A'(θ)/A(θ)]^2=θ μ'(θ). Let m(θ) be the least m such that a_m/a_{m+1}≥θ. Since m(θ) is a mode of the tilted sequence, Darroch's rule gives |m(θ)-μ(θ)|<1. More precisely, assuming a_k>0 and a_{k+1}>0, a_k/a_{k+1}≥θ if μ(θ)≤k+1/(k+2), while a_k/a_{k+1}≤θ if μ(θ)≥k+1-1/(n-k+1). If l and r are the least and greatest indices with a_l,a_r>0 and l<r, then μ(θ) is continuous and strictly increasing from l to r as θ increases from 0 to ∞. Hence, for x in (l,r), let θ(x) be the unique positive solution of θ A'(θ)/A(θ)=x. Then, whenever the displayed quantities are defined, θ(k+1/(k+2))≤a_k/a_{k+1}≤θ(k+1-1/(n-k+1)), and in particular θ(k)<a_k/a_{k+1}<θ(k+1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is given in the paper. Geometric tilting preserves the PF property, so Darroch's rule applies to the tilted sequence. The ratio of consecutive tilted coefficients is (a_k θ^k)/(a_{k+1} θ^{k+1})=(a_k/a_{k+1})/θ, so the first index at which this ratio reaches at least 1 is exactly the least m with a_m/a_{m+1}≥θ; this is a mode of the tilted sequence. Applying the precise Darroch alternatives to the tilted distribution yields the two one-sided implications for a_k/a_{k+1}. The identity σ^2(θ)=θ μ'(θ) shows μ'(θ)>0 on the nondegenerate support, so μ(θ) is strictly increasing and invertible; substituting the inverse θ(x) into the one-sided implications gives the stated ratio bounds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Normal and local normal approximation for PF distributions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a PF distribution on {0,1,...,n} with mean μ and variance σ^2>0. Let φ(x)=(2π)^{-1/2} exp(-x^2/2) be the standard normal density and Φ(z)=∫_{-∞}^z φ(x) dx its distribution function. Then max_{0≤k≤n} |P[0,k]-Φ((k-μ)/σ)| < 0.7975/σ. Also, there exists a universal constant C such that max_{0≤k≤n} |σ P(k)-φ((k-μ)/σ)| < C/σ. Consequently, for a sequence of finite PF distributions with variances σ_n^2, the standardized variables converge in distribution to the standard normal if and only if σ_n→∞; moreover, no other continuous limiting distribution can arise from affine rescaling of finite-range PF distributions.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper obtains the distribution-function estimate from a refinement of the Berry-Esseen theorem due to van Beek, applied after Proposition 1 represents P as a sum of independent Bernoulli variables. The local estimate is quoted from Platonov, with the paper noting that the argument is correct for PF distributions. The central-limit consequence is immediate from the uniform error tending to zero when σ_n→∞ and the standard weak-convergence theorem cited in the paper; the converse and exclusion of other continuous limits use the same uniform normal approximation structure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Coefficient approximation by tilting
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (a_0,...,a_n) be a finite PF sequence with A(z)=∑_{j=0}^n a_j z^j. For an index k in the interior of the support, choose θ=θ(k)>0 solving θ A'(θ)/A(θ)=k, and let σ^2(θ) be the variance of the normalized tilted sequence (a_j θ^j). Then a_k = [A(θ)/(θ^k √(2π) σ(θ))] (1+ε_k), where |ε_k|≤C/σ(θ) for the same universal constant C appearing in the local normal estimate.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper derives this directly from the local normal approximation. Normalize the tilted sequence by setting P_θ(j)=a_j θ^j/A(θ). By the choice of θ, P_θ has mean k. Applying the local normal estimate at the central point j=k gives σ(θ) P_θ(k)=1/√(2π) times a relative error bounded by C/σ(θ). Solving the identity P_θ(k)=a_k θ^k/A(θ) for a_k gives the displayed formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Poisson approximation for PF distributions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a PF distribution on nonnegative integers with finite support, mean μ>0, and variance σ^2. In the Bernoulli representation P=law(∑_i X_i) with X_i Bernoulli(q_i), one has μ-σ^2=∑_i q_i^2≥0. The total variation distance in the paper's doubled convention satisfies ∑_k |P(k)-e^{-μ} μ^k/k!| ≤ (1-e^{-μ}) ((μ-σ^2)/μ). Thus the Poisson(μ) approximation is good whenever μ-σ^2 is small compared with μ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The identity μ-σ^2=∑ q_i^2 follows immediately from μ=∑ q_i and σ^2=∑ q_i(1-q_i). The total-variation estimate is quoted from Barbour-Holst-Janson for sums of independent Bernoulli indicators. The paper applies it to PF distributions through Proposition 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Closure operations for finite PF sequences
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper records that the class of finite PF sequences is closed under the following operations. If (a_0,...,a_n) is PF, then so are its reversal b_k=a_{n-k}, its geometric tilt b_k=θ^k a_k for θ>0, its factorial tilt b_k=a_k/k!, and its binomial-moment transform b_k=∑_{i=0}^n binom(i,k) a_i. If (a_k) and (b_k) are finite PF sequences, then their convolution c_k=∑_{j=0}^k a_j b_{k-j} is PF; also c_k=a_k b_k k! is PF, and consequently c_k=a_k b_k is PF.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For reversal, geometric tilting, and convolution, the closure is transparent from the Bernoulli or generating-polynomial representation: reversal corresponds to replacing success indicators by failures, geometric tilting rescales the linear factors and then renormalizes, and convolution corresponds to adding independent Bernoulli-count variables. Factorial tilting, binomial moments, and product closure are cited from the total-positivity and real-rooted polynomial literature. The paper gives a probabilistic interpretation of the product a_k b_k: if S_n and T_n are independent Bernoulli-count variables with normalized coefficient sequences proportional to a_k and b_k, then the conditional distribution of S_n given S_n=T_n is proportional to a_k b_k; product closure says this conditional law is again PF, although that is not probabilistically obvious.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2: infinite PF sequences and Edrei's representation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (a_0,a_1,...) be nonnegative real numbers with 0<A<∞, where A=∑_{i≥0} a_i. Then (a_i) is a PF sequence if and only if the normalized sequence (a_i/A) is the distribution of ∑_j X_j + ∑_j Y_j + Z, where the variables are independent, X_j is Bernoulli(p_j), Y_j has probability generating function (1-β_j)/(1-β_j z) for some β_j in [0,1), Z is Poisson(λ), and the parameters satisfy ∑_j(p_j+β_j)<∞ and 0≤λ<∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper invokes Edrei's theorem: a PF sequence with a_0=1 has generating function A(z)=exp(λ z) ∏_{i≥1} (1+α_i z)/(1-β_i z), with λ≥0, α_i≥0, β_i≥0, and ∑(α_i+β_i)<∞. After normalization at z=1, each numerator factor becomes a Bernoulli probability generating function, each denominator factor becomes a geometric probability generating function with ratio β_i, and exp(λ z) normalizes to the Poisson generating function exp(λ(z-1)). Multiplication of probability generating functions corresponds to convolution, i.e. summation of independent variables. The finite-support equivalence in Proposition 1 is recovered when the geometric and Poisson factors are absent and only finitely many Bernoulli factors remain.

proof_techniques.name: Real-rooted generating function to independent Bernoulli sum
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the fact that a nonnegative finite sequence with real-rooted generating polynomial can be normalized into a probability generating function that factors as a product of linear Bernoulli factors. This turns coefficient questions into questions about the number of successes in independent, not necessarily identically distributed, Bernoulli trials. Once this conversion is made, probabilistic tools such as Hoeffding inequalities, Berry-Esseen bounds, Chernoff estimates, and Poisson approximation apply directly.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the weighted threshold event can be encoded or bounded by coefficients of a real-rooted polynomial, this technique converts the problem to a Poisson-binomial count problem with available tail estimates. It is especially relevant for reductions that discretize weights, condition on a count, or compare to an unweighted success-count distribution.

proof_techniques.name: Hoeffding binomial extremality
proof_techniques.description: For PF distributions on {0,...,n} with fixed mean μ, the binomial distribution Bin(n, μ/n) is extremal for convex expectations and for tails outside the mean. In the paper this gives a clean route from arbitrary Poisson-binomial laws to explicit binomial tail bounds, because the unequal-probability Bernoulli sum is less spread out than the equal-probability binomial with the same support size and mean.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A solver could try to dominate a difficult failure event by a tail of a PF count distribution and then replace that tail by the corresponding binomial tail. This may help identify whether equal-weight or equal-probability count cases are extremal for the original inequality.

proof_techniques.name: Exponential tilting to move a target index to the mean
proof_techniques.description: Given coefficients a_k, the paper tilts them to a_k θ^k. The PF property is preserved, and the tilted mean μ(θ)=θ A'(θ)/A(θ) moves continuously and strictly across the support. Choosing θ so that μ(θ)=k places a chosen coefficient at the center of the tilted distribution, where Darroch's rule and local normal approximation are strongest. This yields both coefficient asymptotics and rigorous bounds on consecutive ratios a_k/a_{k+1}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For threshold inequalities, tilting can transform a boundary coefficient or cutoff into a central point of an auxiliary distribution. If a weighted-sum reduction produces coefficient comparisons, the ratio bounds θ(k)<a_k/a_{k+1}<θ(k+1) may provide monotonicity or crossing information.

proof_techniques.name: Mode near mean via Darroch's rule
proof_techniques.description: Darroch's theorem states that the mode of a PF distribution lies within one unit of its mean, with sharper alternatives depending on the fractional location of the mean. The paper uses this as a robust structural fact about Poisson-binomial distributions, independent of the individual Bernoulli success probabilities. It pairs naturally with log-concavity, since PF distributions are unimodal with at most two adjacent modes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Many threshold questions at or near the expectation require knowing where mass begins to accumulate. If the original event can be reduced to a Bernoulli-count half-line, Darroch's rule, and especially the median analogue cited by the paper, could certify lower bounds for probabilities at the mean.

proof_techniques.name: PF2 log-concavity to bound central interval mass from endpoint coefficients
proof_techniques.description: The paper records a Bernoulli-type interval estimate for any normalized summable PF2 sequence: the probability of an interval [b,c] containing an index m is at least 1-max(a_b,a_c)/a_m. The only structural requirement is PF2, equivalently log-concavity with no internal zeros, so this is a comparatively low-order consequence of total positivity. It turns coefficient comparisons at two endpoints into a probability bound for the whole central interval.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof creates a log-concave sequence of conditional probabilities indexed by a count or weight level, this estimate can lower-bound the probability of a favorable central region without summing the entire distribution. It may be useful for proving that bad tails have mass at most a specified coefficient ratio.
